questions.question: An interviewer asks you to design a parking lot. The current requirements list three vehicle sizes (motorcycle, car, truck) with fixed flat-rate pricing. A candidate immediately introduces a Strategy interface for pricing with three concrete pricing strategies, plus an abstract VehicleType class with three subclasses, plus a Factory to construct vehicles from input strings. The pricing logic in each strategy is a single multiplication. Why is this a weaker design than a flat Vehicle class with a `size` enum and a switch on size for pricing?
options: The pattern-based design is actually stronger — using Strategy and Factory is the textbook approach for vehicle systems and signals object-oriented maturity to the interviewer.

The pattern-based design adds inheritance and indirection that the problem doesn't justify; the simpler enum-and-switch shape is faster to read, easier to test, and equally easy to extend if a fourth size shows up. Adding patterns to a problem that doesn't need them is the most common failure mode interviewers flag.

The pattern-based design fails because Strategy is meant for runtime-swappable algorithms, while pricing here is a property of the vehicle — the candidate should have used the Decorator pattern instead, which would correctly attach pricing as a wrappable concern around each vehicle type.

The pattern-based design is incorrect because Factory and Strategy don't compose well in OO systems; the candidate would hit a known GoF conflict where the Factory cannot return Strategy-wrapped objects without violating type covariance constraints in the language.
questions.explanation: The flat-rate pricing across three fixed sizes is a one-liner. Wrapping it in Strategy + Factory + abstract base classes adds indirection and reading-cost without buying any flexibility the requirements ask for. Interviewers grade *match between problem complexity and solution complexity*; over-engineering signals you can't tell when sophistication is warranted. The right move is to start with the enum-and-switch and refactor to Strategy only if pricing rules diverge meaningfully (tiered, time-of-day, dynamic).
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'For three sizes with flat pricing, I'd start with a Vehicle class holding a size enum and a single price() method that switches on size. If pricing rules later diverge — peak hours, EV surcharge, valet — that's when Strategy earns its complexity. Adding it now would be over-engineering for a one-line pricing rule.'
questions.proTip: When you reach for a pattern in an LLD interview, say *out loud* what concrete future requirement makes the pattern worth its complexity. If you can't name one, you're over-engineering.

questions.question: You're designing an in-memory rate limiter. The first requirement is exactly one rule: 'no user can do more than 10 actions per minute.' You implement it as a single class with a `Map<userId, slidingWindow>`. The interviewer then adds: 'Now we want different limits per endpoint, configurable from a YAML file, with a fallback to the global default.' What is the right next move under KISS?
options: Refactor immediately to a full Strategy + Decorator + Builder stack so the design can absorb every limit shape that might ever appear — including burst limits, token buckets, and IP-based throttles — before writing any of the new code, because retrofitting later is more expensive than designing for the general case now.

Reject the new requirement on KISS grounds and tell the interviewer that adding per-endpoint rules complicates the design beyond what one class should handle, so the rate limiter should remain global and per-endpoint limiting should be moved to a separate service.

Extract a small Limit struct (name, scope, max, window) and a LimitResolver that picks the right Limit for a given (user, endpoint) pair, defaulting to the global one. Keep the sliding-window mechanics in the single class. KISS doesn't mean 'don't change the design' — it means 'add only the structure the new requirement actually demands.'

Replace the in-memory Map with Redis right away, because once limits become configurable, the design must be ready for distributed deployment — the storage swap is the architecturally honest response to the new requirement and avoids a second rewrite later.
questions.explanation: KISS is not 'never change the design' and not 'add everything you might ever need'. It is 'add the *minimum* structure the new requirement demands and stop there.' The new requirement introduces variability in *which limit applies*, not in *how the sliding window works*, so the right structural addition is a Limit struct + a resolver — nothing more. Option A over-engineers for unstated requirements. Option B refuses the requirement, which is not a KISS move. Option D conflates a storage decision with the structural one being asked.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'I'd extract a small Limit value object — name, scope, max, window — and a resolver that picks the right one for the request, defaulting to the global limit. The sliding-window mechanics stay where they are. That's the smallest change the new requirement actually forces. If burst rules or token buckets come later, that's when I introduce a Strategy.'
questions.proTip: KISS is a *response curve*, not a static rule. As requirements grow, structure grows — but only the structure the new requirements force, never speculative structure.

questions.question: You've designed a chess engine using a single `Game` class that holds the board state, validates moves, and tracks turn history. The interviewer says: 'This feels too monolithic — shouldn't you split it up?' Two candidates respond differently. Candidate X immediately agrees and starts decomposing into Board, MoveValidator, History, GameState, and TurnManager. Candidate Y pauses and asks: 'What specifically is the concern — testability, future variants like Chess960, or readability?' Why does Y's response carry more senior signal even though X's decomposition is technically defensible?
options: X's decomposition is wrong because the Game class is the canonical aggregate root for chess and breaking it apart violates DDD principles; Y is correct only because they implicitly preserve the aggregate boundary by asking for clarification before splitting.

Y's response shows that complexity is added *in response to a stated need*, not in response to interviewer pressure. X capitulates to the pushback by adding structure without knowing which problem the structure is meant to solve, which is the same anti-pattern as over-engineering up front — except triggered by anxiety rather than enthusiasm.

Y is correct because the interviewer's question is a trick — the Game class is fine as-is and any decomposition would violate KISS regardless of which subsystem is broken out. X loses points for accepting the premise that a split is needed at all when the right answer is to defend the monolithic design.

Y's response is preferable mainly because it slows the interview down and gives both parties time to think; the actual decomposition X proposes is reasonable, and the only real difference is pacing — which is a communication preference rather than a technical one.
questions.explanation: Over-engineering under interviewer pressure is structurally identical to over-engineering up front: in both cases, structure is added without a concrete problem it solves. Y's clarifying question routes the decomposition to whatever the interviewer actually cares about — if it's testability, MoveValidator might be enough; if it's variants, an abstract RuleSet is the move; if it's just readability, a few well-named private methods may be all that's needed. The senior signal is 'I add structure to solve named problems', not 'I add structure when challenged'. Option C is wrong because the interviewer's pushback is legitimate input the candidate must engage with, not a trap to defend against.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'Before I split this up, can I check what's driving the concern — is it that the class is hard to test, that it won't extend to variants like Chess960, or that the methods are getting hard to read? The right decomposition depends on which of those is biting, and I don't want to add structure that doesn't solve the actual problem.'
questions.proTip: When an interviewer challenges your design, the wrong response is to immediately change it. The right response is to surface *which problem* the change is meant to solve, then make the smallest change that solves it.

questions.question: A team's `Order` class has grown to 800 lines. Half the methods are tax calculation logic, copied with minor variations from the `Quote` class. A new engineer proposes splitting tax logic into a `TaxEngine` shared by both classes, and adopting a Strategy pattern internally so jurisdiction-specific rules (US states, EU VAT, Indian GST) plug in cleanly. The team lead pushes back: 'KISS — start simple. We can always extract later.' Which response best captures the senior-level move?
options: The lead is right — the duplication has been tolerable so far, so YAGNI applies: extract only when a concrete jurisdiction is actually being added. Premature extraction risks designing the wrong abstraction, and Strategy specifically is hard to refactor away once consumers depend on it.

The new engineer is right — 800-line classes with cross-class duplication are a signal that 'starting simple' has already failed twice (size of class, duplication of logic). KISS does not mean 'never refactor'; it means 'match structure to current needs', and current needs now include a third jurisdiction. The lead is invoking KISS to defend the status quo, which is a different thing.

Both are wrong — the right move is to leave Order as-is but introduce a TaxCalculator utility called from both classes, avoiding both the 800-line problem and the new abstraction. This is the only option that satisfies KISS, DRY, and YAGNI simultaneously without committing to a Strategy implementation.

The lead is right on KISS but wrong on timing — the extraction should happen, but only after the third jurisdiction's code is written and the duplication becomes textual rather than just conceptual; abstracting before the third instance produces a Strategy interface that doesn't fit the third case and has to be rewritten.
questions.explanation: The two-levels-of-indirection trap here is misreading 'KISS' as 'don't refactor.' The actual signal is that the existing class has crossed two real thresholds: a 500-1000 line size (the article's own example) and cross-class duplication (the article's DRY example). KISS is supposed to *grow* structure as requirements grow; using KISS to defend an already-failed design is a misapplication. Option A invokes YAGNI in the wrong direction (the requirement to handle new jurisdictions is real, not hypothetical). Option C is the 'rule of three' canard — by the time you have 800 lines + 2-class duplication + a third jurisdiction incoming, you're past the threshold the article itself names. Option D delays the refactor for a fourth instance that's not needed to validate the abstraction. The senior insight: KISS is a *response curve*, and ignoring the size/duplication signals to invoke it as a slogan is the failure mode.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'KISS doesn't mean don't refactor — it means match structure to current needs. Right now we have an 800-line class, the same logic duplicated across two classes, and a third jurisdiction landing. Each of those is a signal the article uses to say *now* extract. The TaxEngine extraction is paying down debt that already exists, not speculating about the future.'
questions.proTip: When someone invokes KISS to block a refactor, ask: 'has the simple version actually stayed simple?' If the class is already large or the logic is already duplicated, KISS is the *reason* to refactor, not the reason to delay.

questions.question: Three classes — `User`, `Invoice`, and `SupportTicket` — each have an internal method that validates an email address with the same regex and the same null/empty/length-cap checks. A bug in the regex (it accepts `..@..`) is reported. What does DRY predict about the cost and risk of fixing this bug, and what is the structural recommendation that reduces both?
options: Cost is low because all three classes use the same regex string, so a global find-and-replace fixes it; the structural recommendation is to keep the duplication and rely on lint rules to enforce consistency across copies.

Cost is high and risk-asymmetric because each class must be located, reviewed, and tested separately; structurally, an EmailValidator (utility, value object, or static method on a shared module) holds the rule once so the fix touches one place and every caller benefits at once.

Cost is low because email validation is one of those textbook utilities that every modern language ships in its standard library; the structural recommendation is to delete the in-class methods entirely and call the standard-library validator inline at every site.

Cost is high but the structural recommendation under DRY is to merge the three classes into a single `Validatable` base class that owns the email validation, so all three subclasses inherit the corrected behavior automatically without an explicit refactor.
questions.explanation: The maintenance asymmetry is the whole DRY argument: writing the regex three times costs little, but every change after that costs three times as much *and* risks one location being missed. The structural fix is to put the rule in one place — class, module, or function, choice depends on the language. Option D over-corrects by introducing inheritance to share a validation method; that creates artificial coupling between unrelated entities (User, Invoice, Ticket) for the sake of one shared utility. Option C assumes the standard library covers your validation rules, which it usually doesn't (most projects layer their own constraints). Option A is technically possible for *this* fix but doesn't generalize — the next change might be only in two of the three call sites.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'Three identical validations is the canonical DRY case. I'd extract an EmailValidator — utility class or static method, depending on the language — and have each of the three callers delegate to it. The bug fix becomes one change that propagates everywhere, and there's no risk of a stale copy. I would *not* introduce a shared base class just to share a validation method — that would couple unrelated entities.'
questions.proTip: DRY for *utilities* (validation, formatting, conversion) is almost always a win. DRY via *inheritance* for unrelated entities is almost always a mistake — that's coupling masquerading as reuse.

questions.question: A developer notices that the `OrderService.calculateTotal()` and `RefundService.calculateTotal()` methods have nearly identical structure — both iterate line items, multiply by unit price, sum, then apply a percentage adjustment. They extract a shared `calculateLineSum(items, adjustmentPercent)` helper used by both. Six months later, accounting requires that *order* totals must include shipping in the line sum but *refund* totals must not, while also requiring that *refund* totals must round each line down before summing while orders should round at the end. What is the most accurate critique of the original DRY extraction?
options: The extraction was correct in principle but should have used a Template Method pattern from the start, with `calculateLineSum` as the abstract method and OrderService/RefundService overriding it. The accounting changes would then have been routine subclass overrides instead of a refactor.

The extraction was wrong because `calculateTotal` violated SRP by mixing line-item iteration with adjustment application; the right fix six months ago was to split each method into two phases per class (iterate, adjust) rather than share code across classes — and then the accounting change would only touch the specific phase that diverged.

The extraction was wrong because it merged two pieces of code that *looked* similar but represented different business concepts. Order totals and refund totals share textual shape but obey different policies; collapsing them produced artificial coupling, so the accounting change now requires either branching inside the helper (defeating the extraction) or unwinding it.

The extraction was correct and the accounting change should be absorbed by adding a `Policy` parameter to `calculateLineSum` that encapsulates the shipping rule and rounding rule; the helper stays, the call sites stay, and the policy object captures the divergence — this is the canonical evolution of a shared utility under new requirements.
questions.explanation: The article's core DRY warning is exactly this: *textual* similarity is not *conceptual* sameness. Order totals and refund totals serve different business purposes — they will diverge as policies diverge, and the original code's similarity was incidental. Forcing them to share a helper created artificial coupling, so when the accounting policies legitimately diverged the shared helper became the wrong abstraction. Option A doubles down on the wrong abstraction (Template Method makes the coupling formal). Option B is plausible-sounding but wrong: the SRP angle isn't the issue — the issue is conflating two business concepts that happened to compute similarly. Option D is the seductive 'just add a parameter' move — once you start passing policy flags into a shared helper, it's no longer a shared helper, it's a thin if/else with two callers; the abstraction has paid no real maintenance dividend.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'These two methods looked alike but they're not the same concept — order total and refund total are different business operations that happened to have similar structure. Sharing the code coupled them artificially, so when their policies diverged the shared helper became the wrong abstraction. I'd unwind it: each service owns its own calculation, and we accept the duplication as the honest signal that these are two different things.'
questions.proTip: Before extracting a helper, ask: 'do these two pieces of code change for the same reasons?' If they change for *different* reasons, leaving the duplication is correct — that's not a DRY violation, that's two distinct concepts sharing an accident of shape.

questions.question: You're designing an e-commerce checkout. Three places need to format prices for display: the cart subtotal, the line-item display, and the email receipt. All three currently call `String.format("$%.2f", amount)`. A junior on your team wants to extract `formatPrice()`. You say 'not yet — leave the duplication for now.' The interviewer asks why. What is the most defensible justification?
options: Three duplications is below the rule-of-three threshold for extraction, so DRY does not apply at this count; once a fourth call site appears, the helper becomes mandatory under standard refactoring practice.

The three call sites might diverge — receipts may need a currency code, cart may need locale-aware formatting, line items may need promotional strikethrough — and extracting now would either collapse those divergences into a single helper with a flag soup or force a painful refactor when the first divergence appears. Leaving the duplication keeps each site free to evolve, and the cost of three identical one-liners is genuinely low.

DRY does not apply to formatting code as a category; standard-library calls like `String.format` are by convention exempt from DRY because they're already abstracted by the language, and wrapping them in a helper adds no maintenance value at any scale.

Extracting `formatPrice()` would couple the cart, line item, and email modules through a shared dependency, violating Separation of Concerns and creating a circular import risk in many language ecosystems; DRY in this case yields to the layering principle and the duplication is the price of clean module boundaries.
questions.explanation: The senior move is to *predict* whether the three call sites will evolve together or apart. For prices on a checkout, they're likely to diverge: receipts often need ISO currency codes for international users, carts need locale-aware decimal separators, line items may have promotional formatting. Extracting now risks the same trap as q6 — three sites that *look* the same but obey different policies. The honest tradeoff is: cost of duplication (three call sites of one line each) vs cost of premature abstraction (a `formatPrice(amount, options...)` whose options keep growing). Option A is the rule-of-three as a slogan, not a thinking tool. Option C invents an exemption that doesn't exist. Option D invents a coupling problem that wouldn't actually arise — a one-line utility doesn't break SoC.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'I expect these three sites to drift — receipts probably want a currency code, the cart probably wants locale-aware formatting, line items might need promotional styling. If I extract now I'll either bake those divergences into a flag soup or unwind the helper later. The cost of three one-line duplicates is genuinely lower than the cost of the wrong abstraction, so I'd leave it. The article makes the same call about email validation in the User class — start in place, extract once duplication keeps recurring.'
questions.proTip: When choosing whether to DRY a small utility, predict the *evolution paths* of each call site. If they're likely to diverge, the duplication is the honest answer. If they'll move together, extract now.

questions.question: An interviewer says: 'You've left the email-validation logic duplicated in three classes. Isn't that a clear DRY violation?' You answered earlier that you wanted to start with the duplication. The interviewer follows up with: 'Wouldn't a senior engineer extract this immediately? You're cutting corners.' Which response best preserves senior signal?
options: 'You're right — let me extract it now. I should have caught it the first time.' (Concedes immediately; quickly extracts the validator before continuing the design.)

'I'd push back gently — I made an explicit tradeoff between DRY and KISS, and chose to leave the duplication until I saw it propagate. If you want me to extract it now, I'm happy to, but the original choice wasn't an oversight — it was a deferral with a concrete trigger condition: extract when the same logic shows up a third time, or when the first variation appears in any of the three sites. The duplication itself isn't a defect; an unmonitored duplication would be.'

'DRY is overrated in modern codebases — most teams I've worked with prefer duplication over premature abstraction, and my approach reflects that consensus. The interviewer's framing assumes a 2010s view of code quality that the industry has largely moved past.'

'The duplication is fine because email validation is a stable rule that's unlikely to change; DRY only applies to logic that evolves frequently, and email regexes have been the same since RFC 5322 — the standard's stability makes the principle inapplicable here.'
questions.explanation: This is the article's exact senior-vs-junior distinction: 'showing you understand this tradeoff is what separates senior candidates.' Option B does three senior things at once — (1) names the principles in tension (DRY vs KISS), (2) makes the deferral *explicit and conditional* ('extract on the third occurrence or on first divergence'), (3) distinguishes a *managed* duplication from an *unmonitored* one. Option A capitulates; a senior engineer doesn't agree just because the interviewer pushed. Option C dismisses the principle, which signals lack of judgment rather than judgment. Option D invents a category exemption that's not real — email validation rules *do* evolve (deliverability checks, internationalized email, deny-lists). The deeper insight: under pushback, the senior signal is to defend a *named tradeoff with a trigger condition*, not to defend or abandon a position rigidly.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'I'd push back gently — I made an explicit DRY-vs-KISS tradeoff and deferred extraction with a trigger: pull it out when the same logic shows up a third time or when any one of the three call sites needs to diverge. The duplication wasn't an oversight, it was a managed deferral. If you'd like me to do it now I'm happy to, but I want to be clear it wasn't a missed catch.'
questions.proTip: Interviewers test whether you're driven by principles (junior) or by tradeoffs (senior) by pushing back. The senior response defends a named tradeoff with a concrete trigger, neither caving nor digging in.

questions.subtopic: YAGNI — You Aren't Gonna Need It
questions.question: Designing a parking lot, the requirements explicitly say: 'flat hourly rate, three vehicle sizes, single entry/exit.' A candidate's design includes: a `PaymentGateway` interface with implementations for credit card, PayPal, crypto, and Apple Pay; an `EVChargingStation` subclass of ParkingSpot; a `ValetMode` strategy hooked into the entry flow; and a `MultiTenantOperator` class to support running the lot under different brand names. Why is this design weaker than one that implements only the stated requirements?
options: The design correctly anticipates standard parking lot features — most real lots offer EV charging, valet, and multiple payment options, so the candidate is showing realistic domain awareness rather than over-engineering.

Each unrequested feature represents a guess about future requirements that adds code, surface area, and maintenance burden today; the most likely outcome is that real future requirements will look *different* from what was guessed (different payment processor, different EV charging API, different brand-licensing model), making the speculative code dead on arrival.

The design is weaker because it adds the right abstractions in the wrong order — the candidate should have defined a `Configurable` mixin first and derived all feature toggles from it, rather than instantiating each feature as a top-level class hierarchy that's hard to disable.

The design is weaker because it violates SOLID's DIP principle — `EVChargingStation` and `ValetMode` introduce concrete dependencies into the entry flow that should have been injected through interfaces from the start.
questions.explanation: The whole YAGNI argument compressed: each speculative feature carries certain present cost (code to read, test, maintain) for uncertain future benefit. The article's specific point is that *guesses are usually wrong* — when EV charging actually arrives the API will be from a vendor you didn't anticipate, when valet arrives the workflow will be different from your `ValetMode`. The dead code then has to be unwound and replaced. Option A romanticizes 'domain awareness' as a substitute for satisfying the requirements actually given. Options C and D substitute one over-engineering critique (wrong abstraction shape) for the actual problem (wrong abstractions present at all).
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'The requirements say flat-rate, three sizes, single entry/exit. EV charging, valet, multi-tenancy, four payment processors — none of those are asked for. I'd implement what's specified. If the interviewer asks how I'd extend for EV charging, that's the cue to discuss design — but not to have already built it. Pre-built abstractions for unrequested features almost always end up the wrong shape when the requirements actually land.'
questions.proTip: In an LLD interview, treat unrequested features as forbidden until the interviewer explicitly raises them. 'How would you extend this?' is the cue for discussion, not for retroactive justification.

questions.subtopic: YAGNI — You Aren't Gonna Need It
questions.question: Designing an in-memory chat room, you choose `List<Message>` for message history. A teammate says: 'We should use an interface — `MessageStore` — so we can swap to a database later.' What is the most accurate YAGNI-aware response?
options: Agree and introduce the `MessageStore` interface with one in-memory implementation. Even one implementation behind an interface is good practice — it documents intent, supports testing via mock implementations, and decouples the chat room from storage details. The interface is essentially free and the future swap becomes trivial.

Refuse and use `List<Message>` directly without any abstraction. YAGNI prohibits any structure that isn't required to make the current code work; the interface is a guess about future requirements and adds indirection that hurts readability now without buying anything today.

Use `List<Message>` directly but keep the storage access localized to a small set of methods (`addMessage`, `recentMessages`) inside the chat room class. If a database becomes a real requirement, those methods are the natural extraction point — but until then, the interface would carry indirection cost without a corresponding benefit. This is what 'design with extension in mind, but only implement what's needed now' actually looks like.

Use `List<Message>` directly but immediately wrap it in a `MessageRepository` class that mediates all access; this gives a swap point without an interface, satisfies YAGNI by avoiding speculative inheritance, and lets you evolve to an interface later if a second implementation ever materializes — getting the best of both worlds at minimal cost.
questions.explanation: The article's exact distinction: 'Design with extension in mind, but only implement what's needed now.' Option C is the operational form — keep the access surface narrow and well-named so a future swap is mechanical, but don't manufacture an interface for a hypothetical second implementation. Option A is the seductive 'interfaces are free' position; they're not free — they spread cognitive load across two declarations, complicate navigation, and lock in a contract before you know what the contract should look like for the *real* second implementation. Option B is YAGNI-as-a-cudgel — refusing all forethought, which the article explicitly warns against ('this principle doesn't mean never think ahead'). Option D adds a wrapping class that has the same indirection cost as an interface without any of the benefit; you've paid for an abstraction you didn't need.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'I'd use a List directly but localize all storage access into a couple of methods on the chat room — addMessage and recentMessages. That gives me a clean extraction point if a real database requirement lands, without forcing an interface I'd have to redesign once I know what the actual storage looks like. That's the difference between designing for extension and building for it.'
questions.proTip: The shape of YAGNI-friendly extensibility is *narrow access surface*, not *interfaces and abstract classes*. Keep the points of contact small; the extraction will be obvious if it's ever needed.

questions.subtopic: YAGNI — You Aren't Gonna Need It
questions.question: Halfway through your parking lot design, the interviewer says: 'How would you extend this to support EV charging?' You haven't built any EV-related code, and you've intentionally kept the design narrow per YAGNI. What is the strongest response?
options: Apologize for not having anticipated it, then quickly insert an `EVChargingCapable` interface and refactor `ParkingSpot` to implement either it or a plain `ParkingSpot` interface. Demonstrating that you can retrofit on demand under pressure is what the question is testing.

Sketch the change conceptually: a `PowerOutlet` value object on each spot (null if non-EV), a `chargingRate` field on the spot, and a small EV-aware billing path that adds energy cost to the parking fee. Note that this is a small, localized addition because the original design didn't pre-commit to a `ChargingStation` class hierarchy that you'd now have to reconcile. This is the article's exact intent: design with extension in mind, build only what's needed now, then *talk* about extension when asked.

Defend the current design and decline to discuss the extension on YAGNI grounds — speculating about EV charging would violate the principle and waste interview time. If EV charging becomes a real requirement, the team can address it then.

Propose a complete new design from scratch that treats EV charging as a first-class concern, with a `ChargingPort` aggregate, a `ChargingSession` entity separate from `ParkingSession`, a `ChargingPolicy` strategy for time-of-day pricing, and a `GridLoad` interface for utility integration; this shows the depth of design you'd bring if EV charging were in scope from the start.
questions.explanation: 'How would you extend this?' is the cue the article *explicitly* names: it's the place to demonstrate that your design is extension-friendly without having pre-built the extension. Option B does the right thing — sketches a localized addition, points out *why* the original design accommodates it (no premature class hierarchy to reconcile), and leaves the actual implementation for if/when the requirement is real. Option A misreads the question as a punishment for YAGNI; it's the opposite — the interviewer is opening a discussion, not failing the design. Option C refuses the discussion entirely, which is a different failure mode (silence reads as inability to think about extension at all). Option D blows up the scope — proposing a complete redesign for a follow-up question signals poor judgment about which questions warrant which depth of response.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'Good question. I'd add a PowerOutlet value object to ParkingSpot — null for non-EV spots — plus a chargingRate field, and extend the billing path to add energy cost on top of the time-based fee. That's a small, localized change because I didn't pre-build a ChargingStation hierarchy that would now have to be reconciled with the existing ParkingSpot design. If the requirement becomes real I can implement it in a couple of hours.'
questions.proTip: Treat 'how would you extend this?' as a discussion prompt, not a coding task. Sketch the change in 2-3 sentences and point out *why your existing structure accommodates it*. That's the YAGNI win condition.

questions.subtopic: YAGNI — You Aren't Gonna Need It
questions.question: You designed a `NotificationService` with no abstraction over the email-sending dependency. The interviewer says: 'OCP would tell you to introduce a `MessageSender` interface so you can add SMS later without modifying NotificationService. Why didn't you?' What is the senior-level move?
options: Concede that OCP applies and immediately introduce the interface; OCP and YAGNI are direct conflicts and OCP is the more important principle for class design, so the right call is to default to OCP unless the abstraction is actively painful.

Argue that OCP doesn't apply because there's no concrete plan to add SMS, so introducing the interface preemptively is the speculative-future-requirement pattern YAGNI specifically warns against. If SMS lands as a real requirement, the interface is a one-method extraction.

Reject the framing of the question. OCP and YAGNI are not in conflict — OCP says 'open for extension, closed for modification' which is about *the shape of the change* when extension happens, not about whether to build the abstraction up front. The right move is to keep `NotificationService` concrete now, *and* to keep its dependency on the email sender narrow enough that introducing an interface later requires no changes to the calling code. The interviewer is testing whether you can defend a design that satisfies both principles by reading them as design *guides* rather than build orders.

Agree with the interviewer's framing and admit YAGNI was the wrong choice here — OCP is one of the few SOLID principles that survives YAGNI critique, because the cost of an interface is genuinely near zero in modern languages and the future swap cost is genuinely high without it; the call should default to OCP.
questions.explanation: The framing-is-wrong move: the interviewer presents OCP and YAGNI as if they're in tension, but the article's own treatment shows they're not. OCP is about *what changes when extension happens* (you add a new class, you don't modify the old one). YAGNI is about *whether to introduce abstractions before you have a concrete reason*. You can satisfy both by keeping the dependency narrow (a single method call, not a sprawl of email-specific calls) so that introducing the `MessageSender` interface *when SMS actually lands* is a mechanical change that doesn't touch `NotificationService` semantics. Option A treats OCP as a default-to-on rule, ignoring its cost. Option B is correct in direction but doesn't unpack *why* OCP and YAGNI both pass — it just picks YAGNI. Option D is wrong on the cost claim ('interfaces are free') and capitulates to a false dichotomy. The deeper insight: principles are tools for thinking; when they appear to conflict, the resolution is usually that one of them was being misapplied, not that you have to pick.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'I'd push back on the framing. OCP and YAGNI aren't in conflict here — OCP describes what should happen when extension occurs (add a new class, don't modify the old one), and YAGNI describes when to build the abstraction (only when there's a real reason). If I keep NotificationService's dependency on the email sender to a single narrow call, then when SMS arrives I can extract a one-method MessageSender interface, write SmsSender, and inject it — without modifying any existing logic. Both principles pass.'
questions.proTip: When an interviewer poses two principles as if they conflict, the senior move is usually to show they don't — one is being misapplied. Resolve the apparent conflict by separating *design intent* from *build order*.

questions.question: A `PlayerProfile` class has a method `renderProfileHtml()` that pulls the player's stats from the database, formats them as HTML strings with inline `<b>` tags around personal records, and writes the resulting HTML to a file. Three teams need to change something about player profiles next quarter: the design team wants different HTML styling, the analytics team wants a new stat on the page, and the storage team wants to move from filesystem to S3. What is the structural cost of this design?
options: Each team's change requires editing the same `renderProfileHtml` method; their pull requests will conflict, their tests will entangle, and a regression in one team's change can break the others. The class is doing rendering, data access, and storage in one place, so a change driven by any one of those three concerns forces touching the others' code.

The design's main cost is performance — pulling from the database inside a method that also does HTML formatting blocks the rendering thread on I/O, so the team should extract the database call into an async preloader while keeping HTML and storage logic together for cohesion.

The cost is naming hygiene — `renderProfileHtml` should be split into `getProfileHtml` and `saveProfileHtml` so the interface signals the side effect; the underlying coupling between rendering, data access, and storage is acceptable as long as the method names are accurate.

The design is acceptable because all three changes are inside a single class, which means version control and code ownership are simple; SoC would only become important if the changes were spread across multiple classes that needed to evolve independently.
questions.explanation: The article's tic-tac-toe example compressed: when one method mixes data access, presentation, and storage, every change driven by any one of those concerns has to traverse the other two. The teams collide on the same file, the tests entangle (you can't unit-test rendering without a DB and a filesystem), and changes to one concern can silently break the others. Option B substitutes a performance critique for the structural one. Option C treats SoC as a naming problem rather than a structural one. Option D inverts the principle — colocating concerns *inside* a class is exactly the failure mode SoC names; the boundary doesn't have to be class-vs-class to matter.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'This class mixes data access, presentation, and storage. A change driven by any one of those — a new stat, a styling change, an S3 migration — forces editing code from the other two. I'd split it into a PlayerStatsRepository for data, a ProfileHtmlRenderer for presentation, and a ProfileWriter for storage. Each team can then change one thing without touching the others, and each piece is independently testable.'
questions.proTip: When you spot a method whose name uses three verbs (`renderProfileHtml` → render + format + save), that's almost always SoC failure. Split until each method's name uses one verb.

questions.question: In a tic-tac-toe game, a candidate proposes a `Game` class that owns a `Board`, a `Display`, and an `InputHandler` — passing them in via the constructor. The interviewer asks: 'What concrete benefit does this layering give you that a single `Game` class with all three responsibilities wouldn't have?' What is the strongest concrete answer?
options: It allows the game to support more than two players in the future without restructuring; a multi-player variant can subclass `Game` and override the turn-management logic without touching `Board` or `Display`.

It improves performance by allowing the renderer to run on a separate thread from the input handler, since they're now independent objects; the single-class design would force them onto the main event loop and degrade frame rate.

It lets you swap any one of those three implementations without changing the others. Console input → GUI input is a change to `InputHandler` only. Text board → graphical board is a change to `Display` only. New win condition is a change to `Board` only. Each change is isolated to one class, and each class can be unit-tested independently with stub implementations of the other two.

It satisfies SOLID's DIP principle by injecting dependencies through the constructor; the main benefit is that the design becomes inversion-of-control compliant, which makes it compatible with standard DI frameworks.
questions.explanation: The article's exact case study: 'If you want to switch from console input to a GUI, you only touch InputHandler. If you want to change how the board displays, you only modify Display. If you need to add new win conditions, you only update Board.' Option A is a different benefit (extensibility for variants) that the layering does also enable, but it's not the *primary* concrete benefit the article cites. Option B invents a concurrency benefit that's not what SoC is about and would be a non-improvement on the single thread that tic-tac-toe runs on. Option D conflates SoC with DIP — they're related but distinct; SoC is about *what* lives where (responsibilities), DIP is about *which direction* the dependencies point (abstractions vs concretes).
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'Each of those three classes can change independently of the others. Console-to-GUI is an InputHandler change. New display style is a Display change. New win condition is a Board change. None of those changes leak across the boundary. And because each class only needs the others through small interfaces, I can unit-test the Board with a stub Display and stub InputHandler without standing up the real ones.'
questions.proTip: When an interviewer asks 'what does this layering buy you?', list specific changes that become single-class edits. That's the operational form of SoC's value.

questions.question: You've split a tic-tac-toe game into `Board`, `Display`, and `InputHandler`. The interviewer asks: 'Doesn't `Display.render(board)` couple the renderer to the board's internal structure? You're passing the whole board into the renderer, so a change to how the board stores its state (2D array → bitboard) would force a change in the renderer.' What is the most accurate response?
options: The interviewer is right and the design is broken — `Display.render(board)` should instead receive a pre-computed `RenderModel` produced by `Board.toRenderModel()`, which keeps the storage representation hidden from the renderer entirely. Without that intermediate model, the SoC split is illusory.

The interviewer is right that there's coupling, and the fix is to define a small read-only interface on the board — something like `BoardView` exposing `getCell(row, col)` and `getDimensions()` — that the renderer depends on. The board's storage representation can change freely behind that interface; the renderer only knows about cells and dimensions, not arrays or bitboards.

The interviewer is wrong — passing the whole board into the renderer is fine because the board's public methods are the abstraction; even if the storage changes from a 2D array to a bitboard, as long as `Board.getCell(row, col)` keeps the same signature the renderer is unaffected. The coupling is to the *method surface*, which is exactly what objects are for.

The interviewer is right and the renderer should instead poll the board through events — `board.subscribe(renderer)` — so the renderer is decoupled from both the board's data shape and from being called explicitly; this is the only way to truly separate the two concerns.
questions.explanation: The interviewer's pushback contains a confusion that the senior candidate should catch and correct: passing an object isn't the same as depending on its internal storage. The renderer depends on the board's *public method surface* (`getCell`, `getDimensions`, `hasWinner`, etc.) — not on whether those methods are backed by a 2D array, a bitboard, a sparse map, or a database. Storage changes that preserve the method contract are invisible to the renderer. Option A is a more elaborate restatement of the same misunderstanding (a `RenderModel` is an interface; the board's public methods *already are* an interface). Option B introduces an extra named interface (`BoardView`) for a problem that doesn't exist — the public method surface of `Board` is already the interface in any reasonable language. Option D adds publish-subscribe coupling that solves a different problem (decoupling *who calls whom*) and is worse for a synchronous game loop. The deeper insight: SoC is preserved by encapsulation, not by parameter shape.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'I disagree — Display depends on Board's public methods, not on its internal storage. If Board switches from a 2D array to a bitboard, the renderer keeps calling getCell(row, col) and never notices. The coupling is to the method contract, which is what objects are for. Adding an extra RenderModel or BoardView interface would solve a problem that doesn't exist here.'
questions.proTip: Encapsulation is the unit of SoC, not parameter shape. As long as a callee depends only on the caller's public methods, the caller's internals can change freely.

questions.question: You've been told a junior on your team treats SoC and SRP as the same principle and applies them interchangeably. The interviewer asks: 'Are they the same principle? If not, where do they diverge in a way that produces different design choices?' What is the most precise answer?
options: They are the same principle; SRP is just SoC applied at the class level, and the article essentially treats them as interchangeable. Any time you split for one, you've satisfied the other. The junior's intuition is correct.

They are different principles applied at different scales. SoC operates across the *system* — UI vs business logic vs storage, often crossing module or service boundaries. SRP operates within a *single class* — one reason to change. A class can satisfy SRP (one responsibility, one reason to change) while sitting inside a system that violates SoC (because the system's layers leak across each other), and a system can satisfy SoC while containing classes that violate SRP. They constrain each other but neither implies the other.

They are different but only in name; SoC is the older term from structured programming and SRP is the OO restatement. In practice they produce identical decompositions, so treating them as the same principle is a harmless simplification that doesn't lead to bad design choices.

They are different principles and SRP is the stricter of the two. Any class that satisfies SRP automatically satisfies SoC, but the reverse is not true — SoC permits multi-responsibility classes as long as the responsibilities are within the same architectural concern. This means SRP-driven designs are always SoC-compliant, but SoC-driven designs may need additional refactoring to satisfy SRP.
questions.explanation: The trade-off-inversion: most candidates treat SoC and SRP as the same principle just because both involve splitting things up. The senior insight is that they operate at different scales and can fail independently. A class can be perfectly SRP-compliant (a `PlayerStatsCalculator` does one thing — calculate stats) but live inside a system that mixes presentation, business, and storage logic across module boundaries (SoC failure). Conversely, a system can have clean architectural layers (UI module, business module, storage module) but contain a god-class inside the business module that does ten things at once (SRP failure). They constrain each other but neither implies the other. Option A is the junior's misconception. Option C is wrong on the history and dangerously wrong on practice. Option D invents a containment relationship that doesn't hold — SRP at the class level says nothing about whether the *system's* layers are clean.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'They're related but operate at different scales. SoC is system-level — UI, business, storage as separate concerns. SRP is class-level — one reason to change. A clean SRP class can sit inside a system with terrible SoC, if the system's layers are tangled across modules. And clean architectural layers can hide an SRP-violating god-class inside one of them. They constrain each other but neither implies the other, so you have to evaluate them separately.'
questions.proTip: When you hear someone use SoC and SRP interchangeably, suspect a thinking shortcut. Ask them: 'at what scale?' SoC = system; SRP = class. Most design discussions are clearer once that's separated.

questions.question: A shipping module computes the destination zip code as `order.getCustomer().getAddress().getZipCode()`. The Customer team refactors to support multiple addresses per customer (`getAddresses()` returning a list, with a `getDefaultAddress()` accessor). What predictable failure does the shipping module experience, and what does the original chain reveal about its design?
options: The shipping module fails to compile because `getAddress()` no longer exists, and the chain reveals that the shipping code knew about Customer's internal organization (Customer-has-Address-has-ZipCode); it had to traverse three different object types to get one piece of data, so any reorganization of those types breaks it.

The shipping module continues to work because Java's reflection layer falls back to the new `getDefaultAddress()` method by name similarity; the chain is fine and the refactor is non-breaking thanks to language-level resilience to accessor renames.

The shipping module fails at runtime because `getAddress()` returns `null`, and the chain reveals that the shipping code didn't null-check at every level; the right fix is to wrap the chain in `Optional.ofNullable(...).map(...)` to handle the new shape resiliently.

The shipping module fails because the new `Customer.getAddresses()` returns a list rather than a single Address, and the chain reveals that the shipping code assumed cardinality 1; the right fix is to update the chain to `getCustomer().getAddresses().get(0).getZipCode()` and handle empty lists explicitly.
questions.explanation: The article's exact example. The chain is fragile because the shipping module depends on the *internal organization* of three different types — Customer's relation to Address, Address's exposure of ZipCode. Any of those can change. When Customer reorganizes its address relation (single → list, or address → addressBook → primaryAddress), the shipping code breaks. The right fix is for Customer to expose a higher-level method like `getShippingZipCode()` that owns the navigation internally; the shipping module talks only to its immediate friend (Customer) and is insulated from how Customer organizes its data. Option B invents a language feature that doesn't exist. Option C addresses null-safety, which is a real concern but not the structural one Demeter is about. Option D adapts the chain to the new shape — that's exactly the maintenance burden Demeter aims to eliminate, doing it again.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'The chain breaks because the shipping module depended on Customer's internal structure — knowing that Customer has an Address that has a ZipCode. When that structure changes, the shipping module changes too. The Demeter fix is to put a getShippingZipCode() method on Customer; Customer owns the navigation, and the shipping module talks only to its immediate friend.'
questions.proTip: Every dot in a chain represents a coupling. Three dots = three things you can't change without breaking the caller. Push the navigation behind a method on the immediate friend.

questions.question: A reporting service needs three pieces of data from a `User`: the user's display name, the user's primary email, and the zip code of the user's billing address. A junior writes `user.getName()`, `user.getEmail()`, and `user.getBillingAddress().getZipCode()`. They argue the third one is fine because two-dot chains are 'short.' Why is the third call structurally different from the first two even though it only has one extra hop?
options: The third call is structurally identical to the first two — `getBillingAddress().getZipCode()` is just a two-dot chain that any modern style guide treats as acceptable; Demeter applies only when chains exceed three or four hops or when the intermediate types are owned by different bounded contexts.

The third call traverses to a different object (`Address`) and then asks *that* object for data; the reporting service now depends on `Address` having a `getZipCode()` method and on `User` keeping its billing address as a navigable property. The first two calls only depend on `User`'s public surface. Hop count isn't the metric — *whether you traverse to an object you don't own* is the metric.

The third call is worse only because zip codes are strings and Java's String hashing is slower than the integer ID lookup the first two calls would use internally; the structural pattern is fine but the performance cost makes it worth pushing into a single accessor.

The third call is worse because `Address` is mutable in most domain models, so the reporting service might receive a stale zip code if the user updates their address mid-report; the right fix is to clone the address before reading from it, not to introduce a new accessor on User.
questions.explanation: The misconception the question targets is treating Demeter as a *hop-count* rule when it's actually a *coupling-direction* rule. `user.getName()` and `user.getEmail()` couple the reporting service to User's public method surface — that's the legitimate kind of coupling, the whole reason objects exist. `user.getBillingAddress().getZipCode()` couples the reporting service to *both* User (must expose `getBillingAddress`) *and* Address (must expose `getZipCode`). Now a refactor that touches Address's interface ripples back to the reporting service even though the reporting service has no business caring about Address. The fix is `user.getBillingZipCode()` — the navigation is encapsulated, and the reporting service stays coupled only to User. Option A invents a quantitative threshold that the principle doesn't have. Options C and D substitute unrelated concerns (performance, mutability) for the structural one.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'The first two calls couple me to User. The third couples me to User AND to Address — I'm now depending on Address's interface even though Address isn't part of my business. The fix is to add user.getBillingZipCode() so User owns the navigation and I stay coupled only to my immediate friend. Hop count isn't the rule; whether I'm reaching into a stranger object is.'
questions.proTip: Demeter isn't 'no chains'; it's 'no traversal into objects you don't own.' One hop into a foreign type is the violation, even if the chain looks short.

questions.question: An interviewer reviews your code: `queryBuilder.select("name").from("users").where("active", true).orderBy("name").build()`. They say: 'That's a five-method chain — isn't it a Law of Demeter violation?' What is the strongest response?
options: Concede and refactor — long chains are always a Demeter risk regardless of return type, and breaking the chain into intermediate variables (`var q = queryBuilder.select("name"); q = q.from("users"); ...`) makes it explicit that each step is an independent operation rather than a single fluent statement.

Defend the chain by pointing out that fluent interfaces typically return `this` rather than a new object, so even though there are five method calls, the receiver is the same builder object the whole way through; the chain doesn't traverse to a stranger object, so Demeter isn't violated. The article makes this exact distinction — chaining through *different* object types is the violation, fluent chaining on the *same* type is fine.

Defend the chain on practicality grounds — fluent builders are too widely used to be considered a violation, and code style guides for Java/C#/JS all permit them; the principle has to yield to community convention even if it strictly applies.

Defend the chain on grounds that builders are exempt from Demeter because they're a known design pattern; the GoF book explicitly carves out builder-style fluent interfaces as a permitted exception to the principle, so the call site is correct.
questions.explanation: The article's exact carve-out: 'Method chaining itself is not the problem. Fluent interfaces like builder.setName("John").setAge(30).build() are fine because they return the same object type. The issue is specifically when chaining leaks internal structure by traversing through multiple different object types.' The right defense names the structural reason (return type identity), not just the convention. Option A capitulates and produces uglier code without addressing the actual question. Option C defends the right thing but for the wrong reason — convention isn't an answer. Option D invents a GoF exemption that doesn't exist and would be wrong even if it did (the GoF book doesn't address Demeter at all).
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'Fluent chaining returns the same builder each step — every dot is a method call on the same object. Demeter is about chaining through *different* object types because that leaks the structure of objects you don't own. A builder isn't doing that. If the chain were queryBuilder.getConnection().getMetadata().getColumns() — three different types — that would be the violation.'
questions.proTip: When asked about Demeter on a chain, the question to answer is: 'do all those dots return the same type?' If yes, it's fluent — fine. If they return different types you don't own — that's the violation.

questions.question: Two languages, same pattern: in Java, `stream.filter(...).map(...).collect(...)` chains four calls returning different types (Stream → Stream → Stream → Collection). In Kotlin/Scala/JS, the same pipeline (`list.filter(...).map(...).reduce(...)`) chains across types as well. By a strict Demeter reading, both are violations. What is the senior insight that resolves this with the article's framing?
options: Both are Demeter violations and should be refactored to imperative loops with intermediate variables; functional chaining is a stylistic preference but doesn't survive principled scrutiny, and codebases that take SOLID seriously typically prohibit it.

Both are exempt because the intermediate types (Stream, Iterator, Sequence) are language-provided rather than user-defined; Demeter applies only to types in the user's own domain, not to types from the standard library.

Demeter is about not depending on the *internal organization* of objects you don't own. The intermediate Stream / Sequence types in functional pipelines are not objects whose internal structure you depend on — they're transient pipeline stages whose only contract is the next operator. The caller doesn't query them for fields, doesn't pass them around, doesn't depend on their representation. The article's distinction is between fluent chaining that hides structure and chain-through-strangers that exposes structure; a pipeline where the strangers are anonymous transient stages with no exposed structure is closer to the fluent case than to the violating case, even though the static types differ.

The article's principle is wrong about functional code and applies cleanly only to OO chains; modern Demeter formulations (e.g. those used in Scala style guides) explicitly exclude functional pipelines from the principle as a known limitation of Demeter as originally stated.
questions.explanation: The framing-is-wrong / implementation-specific gotcha: a strict reading of 'no chain through different types' would prohibit every functional pipeline, which would be absurd. The senior reading goes back to *what Demeter is actually preventing* — coupling to the internal organization of objects you don't own. A Stream stage isn't an object you depend on the structure of; you don't ask it for fields, you don't pass it around, you can't even meaningfully observe it outside the pipeline. The intermediate types are pipeline plumbing, not domain objects. The principle's spirit is preserved because nothing leaks. Option A treats Demeter as a syntactic ban on chains, which the article explicitly rejects. Option B invents a 'standard library exemption' the principle doesn't have. Option D blames the principle rather than reading it carefully. The deeper insight: design principles are propositional, not syntactic — `chain.length > 1` is not the test; *what coupling does the chain create* is the test.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'The principle is about not depending on the internal organization of strangers. In a functional pipeline, the intermediate Stream stages aren't objects I depend on the structure of — they're transient plumbing with no observable internals beyond the next operator. The chain doesn't leak structure even though the types differ, so it doesn't violate the spirit of the rule. Compare that to order.getCustomer().getAddress().getZipCode() — there the chain exposes the internal organization of Customer and Address, which is what Demeter actually prevents.'
questions.proTip: When a principle seems to forbid something obviously fine (functional pipelines, fluent builders), go back to the *coupling* the principle is preventing. If your code doesn't create that coupling, it doesn't violate the principle — even if it superficially matches the forbidden syntax.

questions.question: A `Report` class has three methods: `generateContent()`, `printToPDF()`, and `saveToFile()`. The PDF library is being upgraded to a new vendor. Why does SRP predict this single change will be unnecessarily painful, and what does 'one reason to change' really mean operationally?
options: SRP predicts pain because the PDF upgrade requires modifying the same class that owns content generation and file saving; the operational reading of 'one reason to change' is that *each axis of change* (content rules, PDF formatting, storage) should map to a single class, so a change driven by any one axis touches exactly one class.

SRP predicts pain because the Report class has too many lines; the operational reading is that any class over a fixed length (typically 200-300 lines) violates SRP and must be split, with the PDF logic being the obvious extraction candidate due to its bulk.

SRP predicts pain because PDF generation is an inherently I/O-bound responsibility that conflicts with the synchronous nature of content generation; the operational reading is that I/O and computation should never share a class, so the split is forced by execution-model concerns rather than by responsibility-counting.

SRP predicts pain because the Report class fails the cohesion metric (LCOM) — the methods don't share enough state to justify being together; the operational reading is that cohesion-by-shared-fields is the test, and methods that don't co-vary on the same instance variables must be split into separate classes regardless of conceptual responsibility.
questions.explanation: The article's exact framing: 'one reason to change.' Operationally, that means each *axis of change* — what content the report contains, how PDFs are formatted, where files are stored — should map to a single class so that a change on any axis touches exactly one class. The PDF vendor swap is one axis; if it touches the same class as content rules and storage, those teams collide. Option B treats SRP as a line-count rule, which it isn't (some 50-line classes violate SRP, some 800-line classes don't). Option C invents an I/O-vs-computation split that isn't what SRP is about. Option D conflates SRP with the LCOM cohesion metric, which is a related but different formal concept and doesn't capture the 'reason to change' framing.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'The Report class mixes three axes of change: what content the report has, how PDFs are formatted, and where files are saved. A PDF vendor swap touches the same class that owns the other two. Splitting into Report (content), PDFPrinter (formatting), and FileStorage (saving) means each axis maps to one class, so each change is isolated. That's what one-reason-to-change actually means.'
questions.proTip: When checking SRP, list the *axes of change* — not the methods, not the lines. If two axes of change land in the same class, that's the violation, regardless of class size.

questions.question: After splitting `Report` into `Report` (content), `PDFPrinter` (formatting), and `FileStorage` (saving), a teammate points out that 70% of the time these three are used together as a pipeline, and asks: 'Doesn't the split just push the orchestration into the caller? Now every call site has to do `storage.save(printer.print(report.generateContent()))`, which is more code, not less.' What is the strongest response?
options: The teammate is right and the split should be reverted — SRP that pushes complexity into callers is a wash, and the canonical solution is a single ReportService class that mediates all three steps internally; this restores SRP at a higher level (one orchestration class) while keeping callers simple.

The teammate is partly right; the orchestration call IS more code at every site, but that's the cost of decoupling. The benefit is that the *individual* changes are isolated — a PDF vendor swap touches PDFPrinter only, a storage move touches FileStorage only, and content rule changes touch Report only. If callers want a one-liner, they can build a thin orchestration helper (`ReportPipeline.run(report)`) on top — but that helper is an add-on, not a return to the conflated design. The orchestration line at call sites is a one-time cost; the change-isolation is a recurring benefit.

The teammate is right and the split was over-applied — the right granularity for SRP is 'one user-visible feature per class', not 'one technical concern per class'. Since 'produce a saved PDF report' is one user-visible feature, it belongs in one class regardless of how many internal concerns it touches.

The teammate is wrong because callers already had to coordinate the three steps internally inside the old Report class; moving that coordination outside doesn't change its quantity, only its location. The split is therefore strictly better with no caller-side cost increase, and the teammate is mistaken about what changed.
questions.explanation: The honest engineering tradeoff: SRP doesn't reduce *total* code, it relocates and isolates *change cost*. The orchestration line at every call site is a one-time write; the isolation of each axis of change is paid out repeatedly over the life of the system. Option A's 'orchestration class' is a real pattern but the right framing is that it's an *optional add-on*, not a reversion. Option C invents a different SRP granularity ('user-visible feature') that the article doesn't endorse and that would conflate axes of change again. Option D is wrong about the caller-side cost — the old Report class hid the orchestration, the new design surfaces it; the cost moved, it didn't disappear.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'The orchestration line at every call site IS the cost of the split. But the benefit is recurring — a PDF vendor change now touches one class, a storage migration touches one class, content rule changes touch one class. If callers want a one-liner, I can build a thin ReportPipeline helper that wraps the three steps. That helper is a convenience, not a reversion — the underlying classes stay decomposed.'
questions.proTip: SRP doesn't reduce total code — it isolates change cost. When defending a split against 'this is more code', point to the *change cost over time*, not the line count today.

questions.question: A teammate proposes splitting your `OrderProcessor` class into 14 single-method classes, citing SRP. Each class has one method and one tiny job. The result is a 14-class graph that reads as a chain of forwarding calls. SRP, taken literally as 'one responsibility per class', seems to bless this. Why is this design worse, and what is the correct SRP framing that excludes it?
options: The design is fine and faithful to SRP — fan-out into many tiny classes is the standard endpoint of strict SRP, and modern test frameworks make the navigation cost acceptable; the teammate is correctly applying the principle and the discomfort is just unfamiliarity with the style.

SRP says 'one *reason* to change', not 'one method'. A class earns its existence when it owns a coherent slice of behavior that changes for one reason. A single-method class typically has no autonomous reason to exist — it changes for the same reasons as its caller — so splitting that finely doesn't isolate any axis of change. It just spreads one responsibility across N classes, with all the navigation cost of decomposition and none of the isolation benefit.

The design violates DRY rather than SRP — 14 single-method classes will have 14 nearly-identical constructors and dependency lists, which is the duplication problem; the SRP critique is technically valid but the operational issue is the redundancy of class boilerplate.

The design violates the Open/Closed Principle because adding a new step requires inserting a new class into the forwarding chain rather than extending an existing one; the right SRP-compliant alternative is a single OrderProcessor class with a Strategy interface for each step, which preserves the spirit of SRP without the navigation cost.
questions.explanation: The misreading the question targets is a hyperliteral interpretation of 'one responsibility' as 'one method'. The article (and SRP's actual formulation) frames it as 'one *reason* to change' — that is, one axis of change. A single-method class that changes for the same reasons as its caller doesn't isolate any axis; it just splits one cohesive responsibility into N pieces of code that all move together. The *cost* of the split (navigation, indirection, ceremony) is real; the *benefit* (change isolation) is illusory because there's no independent change axis to isolate. This is also where SRP and SoC reinforce each other (the cross-bridge): a class deserves to exist when it owns a meaningful slice of behavior, not just when its method count is one. Option A romanticizes the over-split. Option C substitutes a DRY critique for the actual SRP critique. Option D over-engineers in a different direction (Strategy is its own form of premature abstraction here).
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'SRP says one reason to change, not one method. A class earns its existence when it owns a coherent slice of behavior that changes for one reason that the caller doesn't share. Fourteen single-method classes typically all change for the same reasons as their caller — splitting that finely just spreads one responsibility across many files without isolating any axis of change. The navigation cost is real; the isolation benefit is zero.'
questions.proTip: Before splitting a class for SRP, name the *axis of change* the new class would isolate. If you can't name an axis the original class didn't already isolate, the split is decomposition for its own sake.

questions.question: A `PaymentProcessor` has a single `process(type, amount)` method with an if/else chain on `type` for `"credit"` and `"paypal"`. The team is asked to add cryptocurrency support. Two engineers propose different paths: Engineer X adds `else if (type == "crypto")` to the existing method; Engineer Y introduces a `PaymentMethod` interface and rewrites `process` to take an instance of it. Which OCP-relevant risk does Y's path eliminate that X's path keeps?
options: Y eliminates the risk that adding crypto requires changing existing, working code — the credit and paypal branches are untouched, so a regression in either of them due to the crypto change is structurally impossible. X's path leaves both branches inside the same method as the new branch, so a refactor or shared-helper change can ripple into them. The OCP framing is exactly this: extension via new code can't break old code; modification of existing code can.

Y eliminates the runtime overhead of the if/else dispatch by replacing it with virtual method dispatch, which is faster on modern JVMs by roughly 10-20%; the OCP framing here is mainly about performance under high transaction volume rather than maintainability.

Y eliminates the need for unit tests on the crypto path because interface-based designs are automatically covered by the existing tests of the interface contract; X's path requires writing new branch-specific tests, which is the maintenance overhead OCP exists to eliminate.

Y eliminates the need to deploy `PaymentProcessor` whenever a new payment method is added — interface implementations can be loaded as plugins via Java's ServiceLoader or equivalent, so adding crypto becomes a deployment of a new artifact without touching the processor; X's path forces a redeploy of the core payment service for every new method.
questions.explanation: The OCP-relevant risk is *the possibility of breaking working code by editing it*. Every time you modify a method that currently works, you risk introducing a bug into the parts that *weren't* the target of your change. Y's path doesn't touch the credit or paypal logic at all — adding crypto is writing a new class. X's path inserts the crypto branch into the same method where credit and paypal live; a careless edit to a shared variable, a misplaced bracket, or a refactor of the dispatch could break either of the existing branches. Option B substitutes a performance argument (virtual dispatch is not actually consistently faster than branch-predictable if/else). Option C is wrong on the testing claim — interface-based designs still need per-implementation tests. Option D adds a deployment-isolation benefit that's a real thing in some architectures but isn't what OCP is fundamentally about.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'Y's design means adding crypto is writing a new CryptoPayment class — the credit and paypal logic is never touched, so it can't be broken by the crypto change. X's design puts the crypto branch in the same method as the others, so any edit risks regressing the working branches. That's what OCP buys: extension without modification means new code can't break old code.'
questions.proTip: OCP isn't a performance principle and isn't a deployment principle — it's a *risk* principle. The cost of touching working code is the cost OCP is paying to avoid.

questions.question: Your `NotificationDispatcher` has three notification channels (email, SMS, push) implemented behind a `Channel` interface. The product team asks for a new requirement: every notification of type `URGENT` must go through *all three* channels simultaneously. A junior proposes adding `if (notification.isUrgent()) { ... }` inside the dispatcher's main loop. What's the OCP-cleanest design and why?
options: Add the if/else as proposed — OCP doesn't apply because the urgent-fanout is a dispatcher-level policy decision, not a new channel; the dispatcher is the only place that knows about all three channels, so the policy belongs there.

Introduce a `BroadcastChannel` that holds a list of `Channel` instances and itself implements `Channel`, fanning out `send()` to each underlying channel. Then route `URGENT` notifications to the broadcast channel and non-urgent ones to a single channel. The dispatcher learns nothing new; the policy is encoded by which Channel instance is selected per notification. New code (BroadcastChannel) implements the interface; existing code (Email, SMS, Push) is untouched.

Refactor the `Channel` interface to add an `isUrgent()` parameter to `send()` and have each channel decide internally whether to forward urgent messages to a sibling channel; this distributes the urgency policy across the channels and avoids any centralization of routing logic.

Drop the `Channel` interface entirely and replace it with a `NotificationPipeline` builder pattern that constructs the routing per notification at call sites; this gives the most flexibility and avoids both the if/else and the interface-explosion that BroadcastChannel would cause.
questions.explanation: The composite/decorator move that satisfies OCP cleanly. `BroadcastChannel` is a *new class* that *implements the existing interface*; existing channel classes are untouched. The dispatcher's logic stays the same — pick a channel, call `send`. The selection of *which* channel handles a notification is a configuration concern that can live in a small router or factory, also new code rather than modification. Option A is the modify-existing-code path that OCP exists to prevent. Option C extends the interface itself, which forces every existing implementation to either implement or default the new parameter — that's the modification OCP avoids. Option D throws out a working abstraction for a different one without showing it actually pays its complexity cost.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'I'd add a BroadcastChannel that itself implements Channel and fans send() out to a list of inner channels. Urgent notifications get routed to a BroadcastChannel containing email, SMS, and push; non-urgent ones get routed to whatever single channel the policy picks.
