The dispatcher logic stays the same, the existing channel classes are untouched, and the new behavior is in a new class. That's the OCP-clean shape.'
questions.proTip: When a new policy needs to combine existing pieces, look for the composite move — a new class that implements the existing interface and aggregates instances of it. Almost always cleaner than modifying the dispatcher.

questions.question: An interviewer says: 'You introduced a `PaymentMethod` interface with three implementations. But suppose the credit-card processor needs a special retry behavior that the others don't. You'd have to either add a `supportsRetry()` method to the interface (modifying it) or put the retry logic in a wrapper. Doesn't OCP fail as soon as implementations diverge in their needs?' What is the strongest response?
options: Concede — OCP is a useful guideline but it does break down when implementations diverge in their capabilities; in practice, most teams add interface methods over time and accept the modification cost, treating OCP as aspirational rather than strict.

Disagree — the retry behavior should go in a `RetryablePayment` decorator that wraps any `PaymentMethod` and adds retry semantics around the `process` call. The base interface stays untouched, the credit card configuration becomes `new RetryablePayment(new CreditCardPayment())`, and OCP holds: new behavior is added in a new class, no existing class is modified. The pattern generalizes — any cross-cutting concern (retry, logging, encryption) becomes a decorator stack, not an interface extension.

Disagree — divergent capabilities are a sign that the interface was wrong from the start; the right move is to split `PaymentMethod` into `BasicPayment` and `RetryablePayment` as two separate interfaces, with `CreditCardPayment` implementing both. This is ISP applied to fix an OCP-breaking divergence.

Concede on OCP but argue that LSP is the relevant principle here — adding `supportsRetry()` and having non-credit-card implementations return false would violate LSP because callers would have to special-case based on capability; the retry must be added in some other mechanism but OCP is a casualty.
questions.explanation: The decorator pattern is OCP's answer to capability divergence. A `RetryablePayment` decorator implements the same `PaymentMethod` interface, takes another `PaymentMethod` in its constructor, and adds retry logic around the inner `process` call. The interface doesn't change. None of the existing implementations change. Credit card payment is configured as a wrapped instance. The pattern composes — you can stack `LoggedPayment(RetryablePayment(EncryptedPayment(CreditCardPayment())))` and OCP holds at every layer. Option A capitulates without offering the right answer. Option C is structurally fine in some cases (ISP-style split) but is heavier than needed when the divergence is purely behavioral rather than capability-based — and it requires modifying the interface or call sites. Option D introduces an LSP confusion that isn't really there.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'The decorator pattern handles this. I'd write a RetryablePayment decorator that implements the same PaymentMethod interface and wraps any other PaymentMethod, adding retry logic around process(). The credit card path becomes new RetryablePayment(new CreditCardPayment()). The interface doesn't change, no existing class changes, and retry stacks cleanly with logging or encryption decorators if those land later. OCP holds because the new behavior is in a new class.'
questions.proTip: When implementations diverge in capability, reach for decorator before reaching for interface modification. Decorators preserve OCP because they're new classes that extend the existing interface — and they compose cleanly with each other.

questions.question: You designed an `Animal` interface with `move()`, `eat()`, and `breathe()`. Three implementations: `Dog`, `Bird`, `Fish`. A new requirement adds `Snake`, which slithers (no `move()` problem there). Then a requirement adds `Coral`, which doesn't move at all. A junior proposes: 'We'll just have `Coral.move()` be a no-op.' What's the OCP-and-LSP-aware critique, and what does this reveal about how OCP-friendly designs can fail under future requirements?
options: The proposal is fine — no-op overrides are the standard mechanism for handling capability gaps in interface hierarchies, and they preserve OCP because Coral is a new class implementing the existing interface without modifying anything.

The proposal violates LSP because callers passing a Coral to a function expecting an Animal will silently get no movement instead of the documented behavior; it also pushes us toward the same wrong shape that the Bird/Penguin example warned about. The OCP-respecting *and* LSP-respecting fix is to split the interface — `Animal` keeps the universally-applicable methods (`eat`, `breathe`), and `Mobile` (or `Movable`) becomes a separate interface that Dog, Bird, Fish, Snake implement but Coral doesn't. This is OCP failing under a future requirement that the original interface contract didn't anticipate, and LSP forcing the redesign.

The proposal is fine for OCP but should also override `eat()` and `breathe()` as no-ops for consistency, since coral's metabolism doesn't fit the `Animal` interface either; this is a known case where OCP-compliant additions require multi-method silencing to preserve the interface's universality.

The proposal's underlying issue is that `Animal` should have been an abstract class with default no-op implementations from the start, which would let Coral inherit safely; OCP would then be preserved by adding Coral as a subclass without overriding anything, and the no-op default makes callers' assumptions explicit at the type level.
questions.explanation: The future-proofing pattern: an OCP-compliant design (`Animal` interface, three implementations) can still fail when future requirements introduce subtypes that don't fit the original contract. The Coral case is structurally identical to the article's Penguin/Bird example — a no-op (or worse, a throw) on `move()` violates LSP because callers can't substitute Coral wherever they expected an Animal. The fix is also identical to the article's: split the interface so the universally-applicable methods stay in `Animal` and the capability-specific method (`move`) lives in a separate sub-interface that only the right subset implements. This is the cross-bridge insight: OCP via interfaces requires LSP-safe subtypes, and when subtypes diverge in capability, the fix is interface segregation. Option A gives a no-op pass that the article explicitly warns against. Option C compounds the error by silencing more methods. Option D turns the problem into an inheritance default, which is the same wrong pattern in a different language feature.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'A no-op move() is the Penguin/Bird trap — callers passing Coral wherever they expect an Animal will silently get nothing. The fix is to split the interface: Animal keeps eat() and breathe() — universal — and Mobile becomes a separate interface that Dog, Bird, Fish, and Snake implement but Coral doesn't. This is OCP failing because the original interface contract assumed all Animals move; LSP forces us to fix it by segregating the moving capability into its own contract.'
questions.proTip: When a new subtype can't honor an existing interface method, that's almost never solved by no-ops or exceptions — those just relocate the problem to runtime. Split the interface so the new subtype simply doesn't implement what it can't honor.

questions.question: A `Bird` base class defines `fly()`. A `Penguin` subclass overrides `fly()` to throw `UnsupportedOperationException`. A function `migrateFlock(List<Bird> flock)` calls `bird.fly()` on each member and now crashes when a Penguin is in the list. Why does LSP frame this specifically as a *design* problem rather than a *missing-null-check* problem?
options: LSP says subclasses must work wherever the base class works. The Penguin's exception means a function written against `Bird` can't safely accept any subclass — callers must now know which Bird subclasses are 'real' birds and special-case the rest. The exception isn't a bug to be caught with a try/catch; it's a signal that `fly()` doesn't actually belong in `Bird`, because Penguin (a real Bird) can't honor it. Fixing it with a null check or try/catch leaves the design wrong; restructuring the hierarchy is the actual fix.

LSP frames it as a design problem because exceptions are slow on the JVM (~1000x slower than normal returns), and `migrateFlock` will degrade severely if the list contains many Penguins; the fix is to override `fly()` to return a no-op rather than throw, which preserves both LSP and performance.

LSP frames it as a design problem because the exception type `UnsupportedOperationException` is unchecked, and unchecked exceptions cross method boundaries silently — making the failure invisible at the caller's signature; the fix is to convert it to a checked exception that callers must explicitly handle.

LSP frames it as a design problem only because Penguin shares no flight-related state with the other birds; if Penguin had a `flightAltitude` field set to 0, the override could return successfully and LSP would not be violated despite the conceptual mismatch.
questions.explanation: The article's exact framing: 'When a subclass throws an exception for a method the parent class provides, that's a red flag you're violating LSP.' The deeper point is that LSP is *about substitutability* — the question is whether `migrateFlock` can be written once against `Bird` and trusted to work for every subclass. Penguin's override breaks that trust. A try/catch in the caller is a band-aid that admits the abstraction is wrong; the structural fix is to restructure the hierarchy (split into `Bird` and `FlyingBird`) so that `migrateFlock(List<FlyingBird>)` cannot receive a Penguin in the first place. Option B substitutes a performance argument for the structural one. Option C substitutes an exception-checking critique. Option D invents a state-based loophole that doesn't exist.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'LSP says I should be able to substitute any subclass for the base class. Penguin throws on fly(), so any function written against Bird can\'t trust the substitution — callers have to special-case Penguin. The right fix is to restructure: Bird has the universally-applicable methods, FlyingBird extends Bird with fly(), and Penguin extends Bird without it. Then migrateFlock takes List<FlyingBird> and the type system prevents the bad substitution at compile time.'
questions.proTip: When you override a parent method only to throw, that's almost always an LSP violation. The fix is in the hierarchy, not the override — remove the method from the parent, or split the parent so the unfit subclass doesn't inherit it.

questions.question: A `Rectangle` class has `setWidth(int)` and `setHeight(int)`. A `Square` subclass overrides `setWidth` to also set the height (and vice versa) to maintain the square invariant. A test `assertArea(Rectangle r)` does `r.setWidth(5); r.setHeight(10); assertEqual(50, r.area());` and fails for Square because the area is 100. Why is this an LSP violation, and what does it reveal about IS-A reasoning?
options: It's not an LSP violation — Square IS-A Rectangle in mathematics, and the assertion in `assertArea` is making an unreasonable assumption about side independence; the test should be rewritten to set width and height in a single atomic operation.

It's an LSP violation because Square *strengthens* the precondition of `setWidth` (silently coupling height to width), breaking a contract callers depend on. Mathematical IS-A doesn't imply behavioral IS-A: a Square can fail to be a behavioral Rectangle even though it's a mathematical one. The fix is to not have Square inherit from Rectangle at all — they should be siblings under a common Shape interface, or Square should be a value type that doesn't expose mutable side accessors.

It's an LSP violation only because the override modifies hidden state without notifying the caller; the fix is to fire a `dimensionsChanged` event from `setWidth` so callers can re-read both dimensions, restoring the substitutability via observation rather than by structural redesign.

It's not an LSP violation — Square correctly maintains its invariant and the test is what's broken; assertArea was written without considering that Rectangle subclasses might have constraints, which is a test design flaw rather than a class hierarchy flaw.
questions.explanation: The classic Square-Rectangle LSP problem. The structural insight: Square's override of `setWidth` silently changes `height` too, which means callers who set width and height independently — a perfectly reasonable expectation given Rectangle's interface — get wrong answers. Square has *strengthened* the precondition of `setWidth` (it now requires that you accept simultaneous height changes), which LSP forbids. The deeper insight is the IS-A trap: mathematical IS-A is about *being*, behavioral IS-A is about *honoring contracts*. A Square is mathematically a Rectangle but cannot behaviorally substitute for one because its setters carry stronger invariants. The fix is to break the inheritance entirely — make them siblings or make Square immutable. Option A blames the test for a real LSP failure. Option C papers over the substitutability problem with events. Option D inverts the responsibility — the test is a perfectly legitimate use of Rectangle's interface.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'Square strengthens setWidth\'s precondition — calling it now silently changes height, which a Rectangle caller doesn\'t expect. That breaks substitutability: any function written against Rectangle can\'t trust that independent width and height assignments survive. Mathematical IS-A doesn\'t imply behavioral IS-A. The fix is to not inherit at all — Square and Rectangle become siblings under a Shape interface, or Square becomes immutable so the bad setters don\'t exist.'
questions.proTip: Mathematical IS-A is a trap. Before extending a class, ask: 'can the subclass honor every contract the parent advertises, including precondition-and-postcondition strength?' If not, prefer composition or sibling types over inheritance.

questions.question: Code review: a function processing payment methods has `if (method instanceof CryptoPayment) { handleCryptoSpecific(...); } else { method.process(amount); }`. The author argues this is fine because the special-casing is small and contained. What LSP-and-design red flag does the `instanceof` check signal, and what's the correction?
options: No red flag — `instanceof` is a normal language feature for handling subtype-specific behavior, and explicit branching is clearer than hidden polymorphism for cases where one subtype genuinely behaves differently. The author is right.

The `instanceof` check signals that the `PaymentMethod` interface doesn't fully cover what callers actually need to do — callers are forced to peek under the abstraction to handle CryptoPayment specifically. That's the LSP red flag the article names: callers having to add `instanceof` checks means substitutability has broken. The correction is to either lift the crypto-specific behavior into the interface (if it generalizes — e.g. `prepareEnvironment()` that's a no-op for credit/paypal) or to introduce a sub-interface (`CryptoCapable`) that only crypto implements, then route crypto through a separate code path that *expects* it.

The red flag is that `instanceof` defeats the JIT's inlining optimizations on the hot path; the correction is to use a visitor pattern with `method.accept(handler)` that performs the same dispatch through virtual methods, recovering the inlining without changing behavior.

The red flag is that `instanceof` will fail when `CryptoPayment` is wrapped in a decorator (e.g. `RetryablePayment(CryptoPayment(...))`); the correction is to use a marker interface and check `method instanceof CryptoCapable` instead, which survives wrapping because decorators can also implement the marker interface.
questions.explanation: The article's exact red flag: 'If a subclass forces callers to add special-case logic (e.g., `if (bird instanceof Penguin)`), you violated LSP.' The deeper insight is that `instanceof` is a *symptom* — it tells you that the interface doesn't capture what callers need to do. The correction has two routes depending on whether the special behavior generalizes: if `prepareEnvironment` is a sensible no-op for non-crypto methods, lift it into the interface; if crypto's special behavior is genuinely incompatible with the others, segregate the interface and route crypto separately. Option A normalizes the smell. Option C addresses a JIT performance angle that isn't the real concern. Option D is a real concern (decorator-broken instanceof) but doesn't address the structural problem — a marker interface check is still an instanceof check that says 'my interface is incomplete.'
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'The instanceof check is the LSP red flag — callers having to peek under the interface to special-case CryptoPayment means PaymentMethod doesn\'t fully cover what callers need to do. Two fixes depending on the case: if the crypto-specific behavior generalizes, lift it into the interface as a no-op default for the others. If it doesn\'t, split the interface so crypto callers explicitly route through a CryptoCapable interface and never see crypto behind a generic PaymentMethod reference.'
questions.proTip: Every `instanceof` check on a polymorphic type is a confession that the interface is incomplete. Fix the interface, don't normalize the check.

questions.question: A `ReadWriteRepository<T>` interface has `read(id)`, `write(id, value)`, and `delete(id)`. You have three implementations: `MySQLRepo`, `RedisRepo`, and `S3Repo`. A new requirement adds `AnalyticsRepo` — a write-only repository that ships data to a downstream warehouse and explicitly does *not* support reads or deletes. A junior proposes: `AnalyticsRepo.read()` and `AnalyticsRepo.delete()` throw. What's the senior critique, and what's the deeper LSP/ISP insight that this scenario exposes?
options: The throws are fine because callers of an `AnalyticsRepo` know it's write-only and won't call `read` or `delete`; the LSP issue only arises when callers are unaware of the subtype, and naming a class `AnalyticsRepo` makes the limitation visible enough to satisfy substitutability in practice.

The throws violate LSP and the right fix is a `WriteOnlyRepository<T>` sub-interface plus `ReadOnlyRepository<T>` and `DeletableRepository<T>` — the original `ReadWriteRepository` becomes a composite that extends all three. AnalyticsRepo implements only `WriteOnlyRepository`. This is LSP forcing an ISP-style interface segregation: the original fat interface is decomposed so subtypes can implement only the capabilities they honor, and any function written against a sub-interface gets exactly the substitutability it needs.

The throws violate LSP, and the fix is to keep `ReadWriteRepository` and have `AnalyticsRepo.read()` return a buffered last-write value rather than throwing, restoring substitutability at the cost of a small in-memory cache; this is the standard 'LSP via fallback' pattern for write-mostly stores.

The throws are acceptable because LSP applies only to behavioral preservation of methods that are *meaningfully implementable* in the subtype; methods that have no semantic meaning for the subtype (read on a write-only store) are exempt from the principle, similar to how `compareTo` is allowed to throw for unordered types in Java.
questions.explanation: The two-levels-of-indirection insight: the surface problem is throwing methods (LSP), but the underlying problem is that `ReadWriteRepository` is a fat interface that doesn't match the natural decomposition of repository capabilities (read, write, delete are independent). The LSP fix and the ISP fix are the same fix — segregate the interface so each subtype implements exactly the capabilities it honors. Functions that need to read take `ReadOnlyRepository`; functions that only write take `WriteOnlyRepository`. AnalyticsRepo cannot be passed where reads are required, which is exactly what the type system should enforce. Option A normalizes the LSP violation by appealing to caller discipline, which is exactly what the type system is supposed to remove the need for. Option C invents a 'read returns last-written' fallback that misrepresents what AnalyticsRepo actually does. Option D invents an LSP exemption that isn't real and would defeat the principle's purpose. The deeper insight: when LSP fails, ISP often points at the structural fix.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'The throw is the LSP red flag, but the deeper issue is that ReadWriteRepository conflates three independent capabilities. I\'d split it: ReadOnlyRepository, WriteOnlyRepository, DeletableRepository. ReadWriteRepository becomes a composite that extends all three. AnalyticsRepo implements only WriteOnlyRepository, so the type system prevents anyone from trying to read or delete from it — substitutability is preserved by construction, not by hoping callers won\'t call the wrong methods.'
questions.proTip: When LSP fails because subtypes can\'t honor all the parent\'s methods, ISP is usually pointing at the fix — segregate the interface so subtypes implement only what they can honor. LSP and ISP are the same problem viewed from two angles.

questions.question: An `Employee` interface has 14 methods covering attendance, payroll, performance reviews, project assignments, time-off requests, and equipment access. A new `Contractor` class only meaningfully supports the project-assignment and equipment-access methods. The team is forced to implement the other 10 methods as no-ops or throws on `Contractor`. What does ISP predict will go wrong, and what is the structural failure mode?
options: ISP predicts that the no-op methods will accumulate stale documentation and inconsistent behavior across implementations, slowly diverging until callers can't trust any of them; the structural failure is that fat interfaces incentivize implementations to silently lie about their capabilities, and the cost compounds with every new implementation that has to fake additional methods.

ISP predicts that the no-op methods will be slower than necessary because they still incur a virtual dispatch on every call; the structural failure is that fat interfaces impose an aggregate performance cost on consumers even when most methods aren't invoked, which becomes significant in tight loops.

ISP predicts that the team will eventually inline the Employee interface into Contractor as concrete methods to escape the no-op burden; the structural failure is that fat interfaces drive teams toward duplication of method declarations across subclasses rather than maintaining a coherent abstraction.

ISP predicts that the no-op methods will pass type checks but fail integration tests when callers expect real behavior; the structural failure is that the type system can't enforce capability constraints when one interface conflates many capabilities, so the failures only surface late in the cycle when consumers exercise paths the implementer didn't expect.
questions.explanation: The article's framing: 'fat interfaces force classes to implement methods they'll never use… leads to empty implementations or methods that throw exceptions, which is a code smell.' The structural failure is that the type system stops being able to express what a Contractor can actually do — every consumer of `Employee` thinks it can call all 14 methods, and the gap between what the type promises and what the implementation actually honors only surfaces when a consumer hits a no-op or exception. The fix is to split the interface so Contractor implements only the small interfaces it can honor, and consumers depend on the smallest interface they actually need. Option A is partly true (silent lies do accumulate) but doesn't name the type-system failure that's the core ISP concern. Options B and C substitute performance and code-style concerns for the structural one.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'No-op or throwing implementations make the type system lie. Anyone holding an Employee reference thinks they can call all 14 methods — Contractor passes the type check but fails at runtime when they hit a no-op. The fix is to split Employee into smaller interfaces by capability, and have Contractor implement only the ones it actually honors. Then the type system enforces what callers can expect.'
questions.proTip: ISP is fundamentally about *what the type system can prove*. A fat interface with no-op implementations means the type promises things the runtime won\'t deliver — that's a defect in the type, not in the implementation.

questions.question: A teammate proposes: 'Let's split our `MediaPlayer` interface (12 methods: play, pause, stop, seek, setVolume, setEqualizer, recordOutput, captureScreenshot, etc.) into 12 single-method interfaces — `Playable`, `Pausable`, `Stoppable`, etc. — and have classes implement only what they support.' Why is this technically ISP-compliant but operationally a bad design, and where is the right granularity?
options: It's actually fine — single-method interfaces are the canonical endpoint of strict ISP, and modern languages support them ergonomically (Java's functional interfaces, C#'s `interface I { void M(); }`); the teammate's design is the textbook ideal and operational concerns about cohesion are secondary.

The split is too fine. ISP is about not forcing implementations to honor methods they can't, but the unit of segregation should be a *cohesive capability*, not a single method. play/pause/stop/seek belong together as `Playback` because no real implementation supports one without the others; setVolume/setEqualizer belong together as `AudioControl`; recordOutput/captureScreenshot belong together as `Capture`. Three or four cohesive sub-interfaces, not 12 single-method ones — and the test for granularity is whether real implementations would split along the same lines you're splitting the interface.

The split is fine in principle but should use mixins or traits rather than interfaces to avoid the multiple-inheritance complexity; in languages without trait support, the right move is to use a single fat interface and tolerate the no-ops, since 12-interface composition is operationally worse than 12 no-op methods.

The split should be along axes of mutability rather than capability — `ReadablePlayer` (query state), `WritablePlayer` (commands), and `RecordablePlayer` (output) — since mutability is the most stable axis of variation across implementations and capability splits drift over time as features are added.
questions.explanation: ISP without cohesion produces a different anti-pattern: interface explosion. The right granularity is one interface per *capability that varies independently across real implementations*. play/pause/stop/seek always travel together — no realistic media player supports play without pause or seek without stop — so splitting them buys nothing. Volume/equalizer travel together as audio control. Recording/screenshot travel together as capture. The test the article implicitly endorses: 'would real implementations split along these lines?' If yes, the split is right. If you'd see 12-interface compositions everywhere, you've over-split. Option A romanticizes the over-split. Option C invents a language-feature workaround for a non-problem. Option D picks the wrong axis (mutability) when the natural axis is capability.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'Twelve single-method interfaces is over-segregated — the test is whether real implementations would split along those lines, and play/pause/stop/seek always travel together. I\'d split into three or four cohesive sub-interfaces: Playback, AudioControl, Capture, maybe Streaming. Each represents a capability that varies independently across implementations. A YouTube embed implements Playback only; a desktop player implements all four; a podcast streamer implements Playback and AudioControl but not Capture.'
questions.proTip: The ISP granularity test: would real implementations naturally implement one of your sub-interfaces without the others? If not, the split is too fine — fold them back together.

questions.question: Your `Worker` interface has `work()`, `eat()`, `sleep()`, and `attendStandup()`. You have `Human`, `Robot`, and `Contractor` implementations. After splitting into `Workable`, `Feedable`, `Restable`, and `Meetable`, a junior says: 'Now my dependency injection container has to wire 4 interfaces instead of 1 for every Human, and call sites that need to do `worker.work(); worker.attendStandup();` have to take two interfaces. Hasn't ISP made the call-site code worse?' What is the strongest defense?
options: The junior is right — ISP at this granularity is operationally painful, and the 4-interface decomposition should be reverted; the original Worker interface with no-op `eat()` and `sleep()` for Robot was a smaller cost than the multi-interface wiring complexity.

The juniors\'s pain is real but the answer isn\'t to revert. Each call site takes the smallest interface it actually needs — a function that runs work and standup takes `Workable & Meetable` (intersection types in TS, multi-bound generics in Java, or two parameters). That call site never sees `Feedable` or `Restable`, so it\'s insulated from changes there and from what kinds of Worker can be passed in. The DI container wires implementations once; the call-site benefit (depending only on what you use) is paid out repeatedly. The right reading of ISP is that consumers — not just implementations — get the type-system precision the segregation provides.

The junior is right that wiring is worse but ISP is still the right call because the *implementations* benefit even if call sites don\'t; the DI cost is acceptable as a one-time setup tax in exchange for cleaner subtype hierarchies, and most DI frameworks have helpers to wire multi-interface implementations from one declaration.

The junior is wrong because Java\'s default methods on interfaces let you provide no-op fallbacks for `eat`/`sleep` directly in `Workable`, eliminating the need for separate interfaces while still satisfying ISP's spirit; the segregation should be undone in favor of default-method-based optionality.
questions.explanation: The ISP benefit isn't only on the implementation side; it's also (and primarily) on the consumer side. A function that needs to call `work()` and `attendStandup()` should declare that it needs `Workable & Meetable` (or take two parameters) — that function is then insulated from what other capabilities the passed-in object happens to have, *and* the type system stops it from accidentally calling `eat()` or `sleep()` on something that may not honor them. The implementation-side benefit (no fake methods) is real but secondary. Option A reverts the decomposition. Option C concedes the call-site cost when there isn\'t one. Option D's default methods reintroduce the same lying-type-system problem ISP exists to solve (Robot.eat() with a default no-op is just the original anti-pattern in a new syntax).
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'ISP isn\'t only for implementations — call sites also depend on the smallest interface they need. A function that does work and standup takes Workable & Meetable, not Worker. It can\'t accidentally call eat() or sleep() on something that may not honor them, and it\'s insulated from changes to those interfaces. The DI container wires implementations once; call sites get type-level precision every time they\'re read or modified. Default methods would just put the no-op problem back.'
questions.proTip: When defending ISP against 'this makes call sites verbose', point at *what the call site is now insulated from*. A call site that takes `Workable & Meetable` cannot break when `Feedable` evolves — the verbosity is buying you change-isolation.

questions.question: A `NotificationService` constructs an `EmailSender` directly inside its constructor. You're asked to write a unit test for `NotificationService` that doesn't actually send email. What does this constructor's structure cost you, and what does DIP-aware design replace it with?
options: Cost: the test must either set up a fake SMTP server, monkeypatch the network at the OS level, or accept that emails get sent to real addresses during testing. DIP replaces the constructor with one that takes a `MessageSender` interface; the test passes a stub `MessageSender` that records calls instead of sending, and the production code passes a real `EmailSender`. The test is fast, hermetic, and free of side effects.

Cost: the test will be slow because EmailSender's constructor opens a TCP connection to the SMTP server eagerly; DIP replaces the constructor with a lazy initializer that defers the connection until the first send call, allowing tests to skip the network entirely while keeping EmailSender as a concrete dependency.

Cost: the test will fail intermittently due to SMTP server flakiness in CI; DIP replaces the constructor with a retry-wrapped EmailSender that masks the flakiness, allowing tests to remain deterministic without changing the dependency shape.

Cost: the test pollutes the actual email logs with test data, making production debugging harder; DIP replaces the constructor with an environment-aware EmailSender that detects test mode and writes to a separate log file, preserving the concrete dependency while isolating test artifacts.
questions.explanation: The article's exact testability framing: when `NotificationService` depends on a concrete `EmailSender`, you can't unit test without sending real emails. When it depends on a `MessageSender` interface, you can inject a mock. The cost of the concrete dependency is that the test environment must somehow work around the real side effect — fake SMTP servers, network monkeypatching, accepting real sends — none of which scale or stay correct as the test suite grows. Options B, C, and D all attempt to preserve the concrete dependency by working around its side effects (lazy init, retry, environment-aware logging) instead of inverting the dependency. They might individually solve narrow problems but they don't address the structural one: the service is hard-wired to one implementation, so testing and swapping are both expensive forever.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'Without DIP, my unit test either has to stand up an SMTP server, monkeypatch the network, or accept that real emails go out. With DIP, the constructor takes a MessageSender interface — the test passes a stub that records calls, and production passes the real EmailSender. The test is fast, hermetic, and the production code is identical to what runs in CI. That\'s the DIP testability win.'
questions.proTip: Anytime you find yourself thinking 'how do I test around this dependency?', the answer is usually 'invert the dependency.' DIP turns 'I have to work around X' into 'I pass a different X.'

questions.question: You're designing a `BillingService`. It needs to send bills via email today, with SMS planned for next quarter. Engineer X creates a `BillingService` constructor that takes `EmailSender`, `SmsSender` and a `useSms` flag. Engineer Y creates `BillingService(MessageSender sender)` and lets the wiring layer pick which to inject. Why is Y's design more aligned with DIP's notion of 'inversion', and what does the word *inversion* actually refer to?
options: Y aligns with DIP because the constructor takes one parameter instead of three, which is simpler; 'inversion' refers to inverting the constructor's parameter count from many to one, which is the operational form of the principle.

Y aligns with DIP because BillingService doesn\'t know which channel will be used — that knowledge lives in the wiring layer. 'Inversion' refers to who defines the contract: normally, the high-level module (BillingService) would have to conform to whatever the low-level module (EmailSender, SmsSender) provides. With DIP, BillingService defines what it needs (a `send(message)` method), and the implementations conform to *that* contract. The arrow of dependency is inverted — both BillingService and its implementations now depend on the abstraction, instead of BillingService depending on the implementations.

Y aligns with DIP because the `MessageSender` interface lets the wiring layer add new implementations without rebuilding BillingService; 'inversion' refers to inverting the build-time order — interfaces compile before implementations, so BillingService no longer has to be rebuilt when a sender changes.

Y aligns with DIP because the wiring layer can use a DI framework (Spring, Dagger, Wire) to manage construction lifecycle; 'inversion' refers to inversion of control (IoC), which is a synonym for DIP and refers specifically to letting the framework own construction rather than the application code.
questions.explanation: The article's exact framing: '"inversion" refers to who defines the contract. Normally, your business logic conforms to whatever the implementation provides. With DIP, you flip this: define an interface based on what your business logic needs, then have implementations conform to that interface. The implementation adapts to the business logic, not the other way around.' This is the conceptual heart of the principle and the most-misunderstood part. Option A reduces 'inversion' to parameter count, which is incidental. Option C reduces it to build order, which is a different concept. Option D conflates DIP with Inversion of Control — they\'re related but DIP is about the *direction of dependency on abstractions*, while IoC is about *who calls whom* in control flow (frameworks calling app code rather than app code calling libraries).
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'Inversion in DIP refers to who defines the contract. Without DIP, BillingService would have to conform to EmailSender\'s interface — taking on whatever methods Email happens to expose. With DIP, BillingService defines what it needs — a send(message) method — and EmailSender, SmsSender, and any future channel conform to *that* interface. Both BillingService and the senders depend on the abstraction; neither depends on the other directly. That\'s the dependency arrow being inverted.'
questions.proTip: DIP is not about constructor parameter shape and not about DI frameworks. It\'s about *where the interface comes from*. If the interface is shaped to fit the consumer\'s needs, you\'ve done DIP. If it\'s shaped to fit the implementation\'s capabilities, you haven\'t.

questions.question: An interviewer says: 'You used dependency injection, so you've satisfied DIP.' You disagree. Where is the precise distinction between Dependency Inversion (the principle) and Dependency Injection (the technique), and how can a design use DI without satisfying DIP?
options: There is no precise distinction — DI and DIP are the same thing in practice, and the interviewer\'s framing is correct; modern usage treats the two terms as synonyms because one without the other is rare in real codebases.

DI is the *technique* of passing dependencies through constructors (or setters, or method parameters) rather than constructing them internally. DIP is the *principle* that the dependency should be on an abstraction defined by the consumer's needs. You can do DI without DIP by injecting a *concrete class* — `BillingService(EmailSender sender)` uses DI but the parameter type is the concrete EmailSender, so BillingService is still coupled to the implementation rather than to a consumer-shaped abstraction. To satisfy DIP, the parameter type must be an interface that BillingService (the high-level module) defines based on what it needs.

DI refers to runtime construction by a framework (Spring, Dagger), while DIP refers to compile-time interface declarations; using DI without DIP means using a framework like Guice without declaring interfaces, which is unusual but possible.

DI is the constructor-parameter pattern; DIP is the field-injection pattern. You can do DI without DIP by using constructor injection without `@Autowired`-style field injection, which most modern style guides recommend; the distinction is essentially a matter of which injection style you prefer.
questions.explanation: The article's explicit distinction: 'DIP is a design principle, while dependency injection (passing dependencies through the constructor) is a technique for achieving it. They\'re related but not the same thing.' The DI-without-DIP example is the killer: you can pass dependencies through constructors (DI) and still have those constructor parameters be concrete classes — that satisfies DI but not DIP, because the high-level module is still coupled to the low-level implementation. DIP requires the *type* of the parameter to be an abstraction shaped to the consumer's needs. Option A flattens a meaningful distinction. Option C invents a runtime-vs-compile-time framing that isn\'t the article's distinction. Option D invents a constructor-vs-field framing that isn\'t the article's distinction either.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'DI is the technique — passing dependencies in rather than constructing them. DIP is the principle — depending on an abstraction shaped by what I need. I can use DI without DIP: BillingService(EmailSender sender) uses constructor injection, but EmailSender is concrete, so I\'m still coupled to the email implementation. DIP requires the parameter to be an interface I define based on what BillingService needs to do — `MessageSender` — and EmailSender becomes one implementation of it. DI is necessary for DIP but not sufficient.'
questions.proTip: Anytime you write a constructor parameter type, ask: 'is this type an abstraction shaped by what this class needs, or a concrete shaped by what some other class provides?' Only the first satisfies DIP.

questions.question: An interviewer pushes: 'You should put every dependency behind an interface for testability and flexibility — that's the DIP gospel.' You've designed a small `RandomPasswordGenerator` that calls `java.security.SecureRandom` directly without any abstraction. The interviewer says this violates DIP. What is the most defensible response?
options: Concede and introduce a `RandomnessSource` interface implemented by a `SecureRandomSource` and a `DeterministicTestSource`; this is the OCP-and-DIP-correct shape and it costs almost nothing in modern languages.

Push back by distinguishing principle from policy. DIP applies when the dependency represents a *substitutable boundary* — a component you may want to swap, mock, or evolve. SecureRandom is a stable, OS-backed primitive with no realistic alternative implementation; abstracting it for the principle\'s sake adds indirection without enabling any swap that\'s likely to happen. The article\'s own framing is that principles are tools for thinking, not a checklist; mechanically applying DIP to every dependency violates KISS by adding interfaces that buy nothing. The right test is not 'is there a dependency?' but 'is there a substitutable boundary?'

Defend the design on testability grounds — SecureRandom is already deterministic if seeded with a fixed value via reflection in tests, so DIP is unnecessary because the test seam already exists at the language level; modern Java specifically supports this pattern via the `SecureRandom.getInstanceStrong()` API\'s testing hooks.

Defend the design on grounds that SecureRandom is a final class in the JDK and therefore can\'t be mocked anyway; DIP is impossible to apply here as a matter of language constraint, so the interviewer\'s pushback is misplaced and any abstraction would be cosmetic.
questions.explanation: The adversarial-pushback test: the interviewer is invoking DIP as a universal rule, and the senior move is to recognize that *every* principle has a scope — DIP applies where there\'s a substitutable boundary worth abstracting. SecureRandom is a stable OS-backed primitive; abstracting it for the principle's sake adds an interface no one will ever swap and a test seam no one needs (PRNG-deterministic test data can be supplied without an abstraction over the source). The article explicitly says principles are 'tools for thinking, not a checklist to recite' and warns against breaking KISS by forcing SOLID where simpler solutions work. Option A capitulates to the rule. Option C invents a fictional reflective seam. Option D blames the language for a constraint that isn\'t the real point. The deeper insight: DIP is for substitutable boundaries; not every dependency is one.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'I\'d push back. DIP applies where there\'s a substitutable boundary worth abstracting — somewhere you might mock, swap, or evolve the implementation. SecureRandom is a stable OS-backed primitive; I have no realistic plan to swap it, no testability need that requires it, and abstracting it adds an interface that buys nothing. The article\'s own framing is that principles are tools for thinking, not checklists. Mechanically wrapping every dependency in an interface violates KISS and produces indirection without value.'
questions.proTip: Before applying DIP to a dependency, ask: 'is this a substitutable boundary, or a stable primitive?' Substitutable: invert. Stable primitive: leave it concrete. The principle has a scope; rules don\'t.

questions.part: K — Meta: Principles in Modern Practice
questions.question: A team writing a new TypeScript service is considering a strict OO + SOLID + GoF-pattern style adapted from their previous Java codebase. The tech lead suggests: 'Use composition of small functions and modules; reach for classes only when state is genuinely shared.' Which framing best captures the *why* behind the lead\'s suggestion without dismissing SOLID outright?
options: SOLID is a Java/C#-era set of principles built around deep inheritance hierarchies and interface-heavy designs that were necessary to manage complexity in those languages. In TypeScript (and other modern languages with first-class functions, structural typing, and easy composition), the same goals are often achieved more directly by composition and small modules — applying SOLID mechanically can add indirection that the language already lets you avoid. SOLID still has value when the design *calls for* it (multiple implementations, dispatch over a closed set, dependency inversion across boundaries), but it shouldn\'t be the default shape of every new component.

SOLID is outdated and should be discarded entirely in modern languages — the principles were specific to Java's lack of first-class functions and have no remaining relevance in TypeScript, Rust, Go, or Kotlin. The lead is correct to skip SOLID and any team applying it in 2026 is signaling that they haven\'t kept up.

SOLID is universal and language-agnostic; the lead is wrong, and TypeScript should use the same SOLID + GoF patterns the team's Java code uses, just with TypeScript syntax. The principles transcend language and abandoning them produces unmaintainable code regardless of what features the language offers.

SOLID is fine but the lead is correct that classes should be avoided — in TypeScript specifically, classes interact poorly with the structural type system and serialization, so all logic should be implemented as exported functions; this is a TypeScript-specific stylistic choice rather than a SOLID critique.
questions.explanation: The article's exact framing of the meta-position: 'SOLID principles come from Java\'s heyday of deep inheritance hierarchies and interface-heavy design. Outside of Java and C#, excessive application of SOLID is falling out of fashion. Modern languages favor simpler approaches—composition over class hierarchies, functions over interfaces. Don\'t break KISS by forcing SOLID patterns where simpler solutions work fine.' Option A captures both the historical context and the qualified ongoing relevance. Option B overreaches into 'discard entirely', which the article explicitly doesn\'t recommend. Option C ignores the language-relativity the article names. Option D invents TypeScript-specific class problems that aren\'t the article's argument and aren\'t generally true.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'SOLID came out of the Java/C# era when deep inheritance and interface-heavy designs were the way to manage complexity in those languages. Languages with first-class functions, structural typing, or easy composition often hit SOLID\'s goals more directly without needing the principles\'s scaffolding. I still reach for SOLID when the design calls for it — multiple implementations, closed dispatch sets, real dependency inversion across boundaries — but I don\'t make every component an interface plus implementation by default.'
questions.proTip: When choosing how SOLID-heavy to make a design, factor in the language. SOLID buys more in Java/C# than in Kotlin/TypeScript/Rust/Go because the latter offer composition, structural typing, and first-class functions that handle many of the same problems more directly.

questions.part: K — Meta: Principles in Modern Practice
questions.question: A candidate in a Python LLD interview designs a 4-class hierarchy (`AbstractAnimalFactory`, `ConcreteFactoryRegistry`, `AnimalBuilder`, `AnimalDirector`) just to construct three Animal instances with different names and ages. They explain: 'I\'m applying SOLID — Factory satisfies OCP, Builder satisfies SRP, the Director satisfies DIP, and the Registry satisfies ISP.' What's the most accurate critique of the design AND the explanation?
options: The design is correct application of SOLID to a small problem and shows the candidate has internalized the principles; the explanation is accurate, and the only concern is that the interviewer may not be familiar with this level of pattern usage in Python.

The design over-applies patterns to a problem that doesn\'t need them, and the explanation reveals a deeper misunderstanding: SOLID principles describe *properties of a good design*, not patterns to mechanically instantiate. A design satisfies OCP because adding a new variant is a clean addition, not because it has a Factory. Citing GoF patterns as 'satisfying' SOLID conflates the principles (which describe outcomes) with patterns (which are sometimes-useful means). The right design is `class Animal: name, age` constructed directly — that satisfies all of SOLID for this scope, with zero scaffolding.

The design is wrong on the principle assignments — Factory satisfies SRP rather than OCP, Builder satisfies OCP rather than SRP, and the Director is unrelated to DIP; the candidate would have been correct if they\'d mapped the patterns to the right principles, but the over-application itself is acceptable.

The design is fine because Python\'s lack of compile-time interfaces makes pattern-heavy designs the only way to enforce SOLID; in static languages this would be over-engineering, but in Python the explicit class structure is necessary scaffolding to compensate for missing language features.
questions.explanation: Two failures stacked: (1) over-application of patterns to a problem that doesn\'t justify them (KISS violation), and (2) a category error in the explanation — SOLID principles are *properties of a design* (one reason to change, extension without modification, substitutability, etc.), not patterns to deploy. A simple `class Animal: name, age` satisfies SRP (one responsibility), satisfies OCP trivially (no extension needed), satisfies LSP (no inheritance to break), satisfies ISP (no fat interface), and satisfies DIP vacuously (no dependencies to invert). The patterns add nothing. The candidate is reciting SOLID rather than applying it. Option A normalizes the over-application. Option C accepts the over-application and quibbles only with the principle assignments. Option D invents a Python-specific exemption that isn\'t real.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'SOLID principles describe properties of a good design — one reason to change, extension without modification, substitutability — not patterns to deploy. A simple Animal class with name and age satisfies all of SOLID for this scope without any scaffolding. Adding Factory/Builder/Director/Registry to a problem that doesn\'t need them violates KISS, and citing those patterns as evidence of satisfying SOLID conflates outcomes with means. The principles show through naturally in the simple design; they don\'t require pattern names to be present.'
questions.proTip: If your design includes a pattern, you should be able to name a *concrete future requirement* that the pattern accommodates better than a simpler alternative. If you can only justify the pattern by naming a SOLID principle, that\'s pattern-worship, not application.

questions.part: K — Meta: Principles in Modern Practice
questions.question: An interviewer asks the meta question: 'When two principles conflict — say DRY pulls one way and KISS pulls the other — how do you choose?' What is the strongest senior-level answer?
options: Pick the principle that comes higher in standard precedence orders (KISS > YAGNI > SOLID > DRY in most modern style guides) and apply it; principle conflicts are usually resolvable by precedence and explicit tie-breakers documented in the team\'s coding standards.

There is no fixed precedence — the senior move is to (1) name the principles in tension out loud, (2) identify *what each one is buying or costing* in the specific context, (3) make an explicit trade-off with a concrete trigger condition for revisiting the decision, and (4) leave the decision visible in the design (e.g., a comment, a tracked TODO) so future maintainers see it as a conscious choice rather than an oversight. The senior signal is *managing* the tension visibly, not choosing one side and pretending the other doesn\'t exist.

DRY almost always wins in disputes with KISS because duplication compounds maintenance cost over time while complexity tends to be paid once at design time; the right rule of thumb is to default to DRY unless the duplication is genuinely temporary (under a quarter\'s lifetime).

KISS almost always wins in disputes with DRY because premature abstraction is harder to undo than duplication is to consolidate; the right rule of thumb is to default to KISS unless the duplication has already shown up three or more times across files or across teams.
questions.explanation: The trade-off-inversion: the question invites the candidate to pick a side or a precedence order, but the senior move is to refuse the framing. Principles conflict because they\'re each pointing at a real cost; the choice between them depends on *which cost matters more in this specific context* and *under what conditions the right choice would change*. The senior procedure is: (1) name the tension explicitly, (2) describe what each principle is buying or costing here, (3) make the trade-off with a trigger condition, (4) leave the decision visible. The article frames this as the difference between blindly following rules and applying principles as 'tools for thinking.' Options C and D pick a side and offer rules of thumb that sound senior but aren\'t — they smuggle in the same kind of mechanical thinking the question is testing. Option A invents a precedence order that doesn\'t exist as consensus.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'I don\'t have a fixed precedence. When DRY and KISS conflict I do four things: name the tension explicitly, identify what each is buying me here — DRY buys maintenance consolidation, KISS buys readability and avoids premature abstraction — make the trade-off with a trigger condition, like "start in place, extract on the third occurrence," and leave the choice visible in a comment so future maintainers see it as conscious, not accidental. The senior signal is managing the tension, not picking a side.'
questions.proTip: When asked how to resolve principle conflicts, the answer is never 'X always wins' — it\'s 'I name the tension, describe the trade-off, and choose with a trigger condition for revisiting.' That procedure is the senior signal.

questions.part: K — Meta: Principles in Modern Practice
questions.question: Two candidates explain the same parking-lot design to an interviewer. Candidate X: 'Vehicle is open for extension via subtypes — that\'s OCP. PaymentMethod inverts the dependency on the gateway — that\'s DIP. The Receipt class has one reason to change — that\'s SRP.' Candidate Y: 'Adding a new vehicle size means writing a new class without touching the existing ones. The billing code doesn\'t care which gateway processes the payment, so I can swap Stripe for Adyen by changing one wiring line. The receipt logic is on its own class because content rules and PDF formatting evolve on different cadences.' Why does Y typically score higher even though both designs are structurally identical?**
options: Y scores higher because the description is shorter, and interviewers prefer concise candidates; the structural designs being identical means the difference is purely communicative and X would win in a written submission.

Y scores higher because Y describes *what the design buys you* in concrete terms — what change becomes cheap, what swap becomes a one-liner, what cadence is isolated. X describes the design by *naming principles*, which is a layer removed from the actual benefit. The article\'s closing point is exactly this: 'Focus on the reasoning behind your choices. The principles will show through naturally.' Y demonstrates the reasoning; X recites the principles. Interviewers grade reasoning, not vocabulary, because the principle name doesn\'t prove understanding — the explanation of the benefit does.

Y scores higher because mentioning specific products (Stripe, Adyen) shows real-world grounding; X\'s purely abstract framing reads as academic and signals lack of production experience. The principle naming itself is fine but should be paired with vendor-specific examples to land.

Y scores higher only with interviewers who don\'t know SOLID well; X\'s framing would score higher with experienced principal engineers who appreciate principle-literate candidates and find Y\'s explanation imprecise. The right move is to read the interviewer and adapt — name principles for senior interviewers, describe benefits for junior ones.
questions.explanation: The framing-is-wrong move: the candidate who *names* principles isn\'t necessarily the candidate who *understands* them. The article\'s explicit final point: 'Focus on the reasoning behind your choices. The principles will show through naturally.' Candidate Y describes what the design *buys* — concrete change-cost reductions, concrete swap costs, concrete cadence isolation — which is what the principles are actually about. Candidate X describes the design by labeling it with principle names, which is a layer of indirection that doesn\'t prove the principle is understood; you can map a label onto a design without grasping the benefit the principle exists to deliver. Option A trivializes the difference as length. Option C adds a vendor-name angle that isn\'t the real signal. Option D inverts the seniority gradient — experienced interviewers in particular are the most allergic to principle-recitation in place of reasoning, because they\'ve seen it most often as a substitute for understanding. The deeper insight: principles are what *show through* good reasoning; they\'re not the reasoning itself.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say it like Y does: 'Adding a new vehicle size means writing a new class without touching the existing ones. The billing code doesn\'t care which gateway processes the payment, so I can swap Stripe for Adyen by changing one wiring line. The receipt logic is on its own class because content rules and PDF formatting evolve on different cadences.' Don\'t name OCP, DIP, SRP — let them show through.
questions.proTip: If you\'re tempted to name a principle in an interview, replace the principle name with the *concrete benefit* it delivers in this design. 'OCP' becomes 'adding a new variant doesn\'t touch existing code.' 'DIP' becomes 'I can swap the gateway by changing one line.' That\'s the version that actually demonstrates understanding.
